category|Category:Utsumochi-jinja}} [[JA:宇都母知神社]] {{wikidata link|Q17217329}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Utsumochi Shrine |Image = [[File:Utsumochi-jinjya 1.JPG|270px]]
Torii gate and worship hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |Location = {{jalink|Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513}}{{jalink|Fujisawa City|Q386661}} 3012 Uchimoto |ISO = JP-14 |Latitude degrees = 35 | Latitude minutes = 23 | Latitude seconds = 31.3 | N/S = N |Longitude degrees = 139 | Longitude minutes = 25 | Longitude seconds = 17.7 | E/W = E |Enshrined deities = {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami}}, {{jalink|Wakumusubi-no-Kami}}, {{jalink|Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto}} |Shrine status = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor), {{jalink|Village shrine|Q134917316}} ({{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|former shrine status}}) |Established = Unknown |Main hall style = {{jalink|Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} |Other name = |Annual festival = {{jalink|September 15|Q2846}} |Map = Japan Kanagawa#Japan }} '''Utsumochi Shrine''' (うつもちじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Uchimoto, {{jalink|Fujisawa City|Q386661}}, {{jalink|Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513}}. It is regarded as one of the shrines (minor shrine) listed among the thirteen {{jalink|Engishiki shrine list of Sagami Province|Q11582137|Engishiki shrines}} of {{jalink|Sagami Province|Q1053732}} recorded in the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}'' of {{jalink|Engi (era)|Q1327274|Engi}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}).{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1273537/73?tocOpened=1 |title=''Engishiki Vol. 2 (Volumes 8, 9, 10)'', Nihon Koten Zenshū Kankōkai, Showa 4. Frame 73. ({{jalink|National Diet Library Digital Collections|Q117959850}}) |publisher= |accessdate=2018-09-03 }} === Enshrined deities === * {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami}} * {{jalink|Wakumusubi-no-Kami}} * {{jalink|Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto}} === Origin and History === The exact date of the shrine’s founding is unknown, but there is a record that solemn {{jalink|rituals}} were conducted in the 3rd year of {{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235}} (according to the {{jalink|Kōki era|Q1209728|Imperial Year}} 1119Stone monument within the shrine grounds{{Refnest|group="Note"|The Utsumochi Shrine page (Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office website, viewed September 20, 2017) states {{jalink|495|Q30806}}.}}) (''Nihon Sōkoku {{jalink|Fudoki|Q1194534}}''). Although there are no records of relocation, the same fudoki states that the shrine was located in “Utsumochi-go, Takakura District, Sagami Province.”{{Cite web|Japanese book|author= |url=http://www.kanagawa-jinja.or.jp/search_dtl.php4?jid=573&cd=1206054&scd=&npg=0 |title=Utsumochi Shrine |date=|publisher=Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office |accessdate=2017-09-20 }} In the ''Engishiki Jinmyōchō'', it is recorded as “Utsumochi Shrine, one of the six shrines in {{jalink|Kōza District|Q1202776}} among the thirteen shrines of Sagami Province (shikinaisha).” In {{jalink|Tengyō|Q1327340}} 2 ({{jalink|939|Q23422}}), the spirit of Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto was transferred from {{jalink|Yamato Province|Q907495}}{{jalink|Hatsuse|Q11396531|Hase}} and enshrined together in the {{jalink|aiden|Q135009883}}. In {{jalink|Shōō|Q1327371}} 3 ({{jalink|1290|Q5552}}), {{jalink|Hōjō Sadatoki|Q3136681}}, regent ({{jalink|shikken|Q1045904}}) of the eighth shogun {{jalink|Prince Hisahito|Q715826}} of the {{jalink|Kamakura shogunate|Q736839}}, rebuilt the shrine buildings. Later, in {{jalink|Kan’ei|Q1192913}} 4 ({{jalink|1627|Q6833}}), the local lord {{jalink|Takagi Mondo Minamoto no Masatsugu}} donated 900 tsubo (about 3,000 {{jalink|square meters|Q25343|m2}}) of shrine land and rebuilt the main hall. During the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, the shrine was also known as “Daijingū” and “Shinmeigū.” In {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}), it was ranked as a village shrine under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}. In {{jalink|Taishō|Q329174}} 12 ({{jalink|1923|Q2169}}), the shrine buildings (built in {{jalink|Ansei|Q1327259}} 5 ({{jalink|1858|Q7684}})) were completely destroyed, including the main hall, due to the {{jalink|Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341}}. However, they were fully restored three years later in Taishō 15 ({{jalink|1926|Q5259}}) September, and remain as they are today. The temple bell at the {{jalink|bell tower|Q1363585}} located just across the road west of the {{jalink|precincts|Q11429002}} was offered in the spring of {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 20 ({{jalink|1945|Q5240}}) for material collection during the {{jalink|Pacific War|Q184425}}, and was not installed for some time, but was rebuilt in {{jalink|1975|Q2479}}. The entire shrine grounds were designated as a scenic forest, and on March 15, {{jalink|1974|Q2478}}, it was designated as a {{jalink|Natural Environment Conservation Area|Q11612866}}.{{cite web|publisher=Kanagawa Prefecture|url=https://www.pref.kanagawa.jp/docs/t4i/cnt/f10578/p34320.html|title=Designation status of Kanagawa Prefecture Natural Environment Conservation Areas|accessdate=2024-12-05}} === Auxiliary shrines === Within the precincts, in addition to the main shrine, there are the following {{jalink|auxiliary shrines}}: * {{jalink|Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247}} * {{jalink|Inari Shrine|Q514480}} === Festivals === * {{jalink|January 1|Q2150}} - {{jalink|Saitan-sai|Q11546824}} * {{jalink|September 15|Q2846}} - '''{{jalink|Reitaisai|Q11546824}}''' * The Sunday before {{jalink|November 15|Q2982}} - {{jalink|Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387}} Festival * Last Sunday of November - {{jalink|Niiname-sai|Q11501518}} ({{jalink|Harvest festival|Q2304773}}) * {{jalink|December 30|Q2901}} - {{jalink|Ōharae|Q11261910}} === Reservoir === At 2009 Ōdaira, Uchimoto, there is a shrine-owned {{jalink|reservoir|Q112083751}} (about 150 m2). === Origin of the Place Name === One theory regarding the origin of the local place name (Uchimoto) is that it derived from “Uzumochi” (宇豆毛遅) of Utsumochi Shrine, which became corrupted into “Uchimodori.” In {{jalink|archaic language|Q11411592}}, both “utsu” and “mochi” are said to mean “small {{jalink|basin|Q813672}},” and it is believed that the area was first called “Utsumochi” due to its topography, which later changed into “Uchimodori.” Another theory holds that when the swordsmith {{jalink|Masamune|Q708935|Gorō Masamune}} from {{jalink|Ebina}} was traveling to {{jalink|Kamakura|Q10939227}}, he rested in this area and, dissatisfied with the sword he was about to present, “returned home to reforge it,” which became the origin of the place name.{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=http://www.city.fujisawa.kanagawa.jp/gosho-c/data15331.shtml |title=History of Goshomi: Origin of Place Names |accessdate=August 29, 2013 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140821214356/http://www.city.fujisawa.kanagawa.jp/gosho-c/data15331.shtml |archivedate=August 21, 2014 }} - {{jalink|Fujisawa City|Q386661}}[Story of Uchimoto] (Fujisawa City Machizukuri Association) === Access === * From the west exit of {{jalink|Shōnandai Station|Q1036357}} on the {{jalink|Odakyu Enoshima Line|Q1063924}}, {{jalink|Sotetsu Izumino Line|Q846367}}, or {{jalink|Yokohama Municipal Subway Blue Line|Q841360}}: ** {{jalink|Kanagawa Chūō Kōtsū|Q10854960|Kanachu}} Bus ({{jalink|Shō19|Q11588892}}): For Ayase Depot via Keio University and Miyahara; get off at “Utsumochi Shrine Entrance,” 3-minute walk. ** Kanachu Bus ({{jalink|Shō25|Q11588892}}): Twin Liner Express for Keio University; get off at “Keio Junior and Senior High School,” 10-minute walk. * From the north exit of {{jalink|Tsujidō Station|Q1049779}} on the {{jalink|East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|JR}} line: ** Kanachu Bus ({{jalink|Tsuji34|Q11588892}}): For Keio University; get off at “Keio University,” 15-minute walk. === Notes === There are often no staff at the {{jalink|shrine office|Q845945}}, and telephone inquiries may not be possible. If you wish to receive a {{jalink|goshuin|Q7504772}}, it is more reliable to visit during festivals or New Year when events are being held. There are also {{jalink|toilets}} installed within the shrine grounds. === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Further Reading === * Utsumochi Shrine “Shrine Bulletin Board,” Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office “Utsumochi Shrine” page, etc. === Related Articles === {{Wikicommons|Category:Utsumochi-jinja}} * {{jalink|List of Engishiki shrines in Sagami Province|Q11582137}} * {{jalink|List of shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture|Q11588941}} === External Links === * https://utsumochi-jinja.com/ Utsumochi Shrine Official Website * http://www.kanagawa-jinja.or.jp/search_dtl.php4?jid=573&cd=1206054&scd=&npg=0 Utsumochi Shrine (Kanagawa Prefecture {{jalink|Shrine Office|Q11590703}}) {{shinto-stub}} {{Shinto}} {{DEFAULTSORT:utsumochi shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Utsumochi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Utsumochi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Sagami Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:藤沢市の神社]] [[Category:相模国の式内社]] [[Category:神奈川県の旧郷社]] [[Category:Need translation]] [[Category:kkkkkkkkkkkkkk]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qq]]

Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 4021005000153 == Commons category (P373) == * Utsumochi-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '神奈川県藤沢市打戻3012', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Utsumochi-jinja}} [[JA:宇都母知神社]] {{wikidata link|Q17217329}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Utsumochi Shrine |Image = [[File:Utsumochi-jinjya 1.JPG|270px]]
Torii gate and worship hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |Location = {{jalink|Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513}}{{jalink|Fujisawa City|Q386661}} 3012 Uchimoto |ISO = JP-14 |Latitude degrees = 35 | Latitude minutes = 23 | Latitude seconds = 31.3 | N/S = N |Longitude degrees = 139 | Longitude minutes = 25 | Longitude seconds = 17.7 | E/W = E |Enshrined deities = {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami}}, {{jalink|Wakumusubi-no-Kami}}, {{jalink|Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto}} |Shrine status = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor), {{jalink|Village shrine|Q134917316}} ({{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|former shrine status}}) |Established = Unknown |Main hall style = {{jalink|Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} |Other name = |Annual festival = {{jalink|September 15|Q2846}} |Map = Japan Kanagawa#Japan }} '''Utsumochi Shrine''' (うつもちじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Uchimoto, {{jalink|Fujisawa City|Q386661}}, {{jalink|Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513}}. It is regarded as one of the shrines (minor shrine) listed among the thirteen {{jalink|Engishiki shrine list of Sagami Province|Q11582137|Engishiki shrines}} of {{jalink|Sagami Province|Q1053732}} recorded in the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}'' of {{jalink|Engi (era)|Q1327274|Engi}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}).{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1273537/73?tocOpened=1 |title=''Engishiki Vol. 2 (Volumes 8, 9, 10)'', Nihon Koten Zenshū Kankōkai, Showa 4. Frame 73. ({{jalink|National Diet Library Digital Collections|Q117959850}}) |publisher= |accessdate=2018-09-03 }} === Enshrined deities === * {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami}} * {{jalink|Wakumusubi-no-Kami}} * {{jalink|Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto}} === Origin and History === The exact date of the shrine’s founding is unknown, but there is a record that solemn {{jalink|rituals}} were conducted in the 3rd year of {{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235}} (according to the {{jalink|Kōki era|Q1209728|Imperial Year}} 1119Stone monument within the shrine grounds{{Refnest|group="Note"|The Utsumochi Shrine page (Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office website, viewed September 20, 2017) states {{jalink|495|Q30806}}.}}) (''Nihon Sōkoku {{jalink|Fudoki|Q1194534}}''). Although there are no records of relocation, the same fudoki states that the shrine was located in “Utsumochi-go, Takakura District, Sagami Province.”{{Cite web|Japanese book|author= |url=http://www.kanagawa-jinja.or.jp/search_dtl.php4?jid=573&cd=1206054&scd=&npg=0 |title=Utsumochi Shrine |date=|publisher=Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office |accessdate=2017-09-20 }} In the ''Engishiki Jinmyōchō'', it is recorded as “Utsumochi Shrine, one of the six shrines in {{jalink|Kōza District|Q1202776}} among the thirteen shrines of Sagami Province (shikinaisha).” In {{jalink|Tengyō|Q1327340}} 2 ({{jalink|939|Q23422}}), the spirit of Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto was transferred from {{jalink|Yamato Province|Q907495}}{{jalink|Hatsuse|Q11396531|Hase}} and enshrined together in the {{jalink|aiden|Q135009883}}. In {{jalink|Shōō|Q1327371}} 3 ({{jalink|1290|Q5552}}), {{jalink|Hōjō Sadatoki|Q3136681}}, regent ({{jalink|shikken|Q1045904}}) of the eighth shogun {{jalink|Prince Hisahito|Q715826}} of the {{jalink|Kamakura shogunate|Q736839}}, rebuilt the shrine buildings. Later, in {{jalink|Kan’ei|Q1192913}} 4 ({{jalink|1627|Q6833}}), the local lord {{jalink|Takagi Mondo Minamoto no Masatsugu}} donated 900 tsubo (about 3,000 {{jalink|square meters|Q25343|m2}}) of shrine land and rebuilt the main hall. During the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, the shrine was also known as “Daijingū” and “Shinmeigū.” In {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}), it was ranked as a village shrine under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}. In {{jalink|Taishō|Q329174}} 12 ({{jalink|1923|Q2169}}), the shrine buildings (built in {{jalink|Ansei|Q1327259}} 5 ({{jalink|1858|Q7684}})) were completely destroyed, including the main hall, due to the {{jalink|Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341}}. However, they were fully restored three years later in Taishō 15 ({{jalink|1926|Q5259}}) September, and remain as they are today. The temple bell at the {{jalink|bell tower|Q1363585}} located just across the road west of the {{jalink|precincts|Q11429002}} was offered in the spring of {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 20 ({{jalink|1945|Q5240}}) for material collection during the {{jalink|Pacific War|Q184425}}, and was not installed for some time, but was rebuilt in {{jalink|1975|Q2479}}. The entire shrine grounds were designated as a scenic forest, and on March 15, {{jalink|1974|Q2478}}, it was designated as a {{jalink|Natural Environment Conservation Area|Q11612866}}.{{cite web|publisher=Kanagawa Prefecture|url=https://www.pref.kanagawa.jp/docs/t4i/cnt/f10578/p34320.html|title=Designation status of Kanagawa Prefecture Natural Environment Conservation Areas|accessdate=2024-12-05}} === Auxiliary shrines === Within the precincts, in addition to the main shrine, there are the following {{jalink|auxiliary shrines}}: * {{jalink|Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247}} * {{jalink|Inari Shrine|Q514480}} === Festivals === * {{jalink|January 1|Q2150}} - {{jalink|Saitan-sai|Q11546824}} * {{jalink|September 15|Q2846}} - '''{{jalink|Reitaisai|Q11546824}}''' * The Sunday before {{jalink|November 15|Q2982}} - {{jalink|Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387}} Festival * Last Sunday of November - {{jalink|Niiname-sai|Q11501518}} ({{jalink|Harvest festival|Q2304773}}) * {{jalink|December 30|Q2901}} - {{jalink|Ōharae|Q11261910}} === Reservoir === At 2009 Ōdaira, Uchimoto, there is a shrine-owned {{jalink|reservoir|Q112083751}} (about 150 m2). === Origin of the Place Name === One theory regarding the origin of the local place name (Uchimoto) is that it derived from “Uzumochi” (宇豆毛遅) of Utsumochi Shrine, which became corrupted into “Uchimodori.” In {{jalink|archaic language|Q11411592}}, both “utsu” and “mochi” are said to mean “small {{jalink|basin|Q813672}},” and it is believed that the area was first called “Utsumochi” due to its topography, which later changed into “Uchimodori.” Another theory holds that when the swordsmith {{jalink|Masamune|Q708935|Gorō Masamune}} from {{jalink|Ebina}} was traveling to {{jalink|Kamakura|Q10939227}}, he rested in this area and, dissatisfied with the sword he was about to present, “returned home to reforge it,” which became the origin of the place name.{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=http://www.city.fujisawa.kanagawa.jp/gosho-c/data15331.shtml |title=History of Goshomi: Origin of Place Names |accessdate=August 29, 2013 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140821214356/http://www.city.fujisawa.kanagawa.jp/gosho-c/data15331.shtml |archivedate=August 21, 2014 }} - {{jalink|Fujisawa City|Q386661}}[Story of Uchimoto] (Fujisawa City Machizukuri Association) === Access === * From the west exit of {{jalink|Shōnandai Station|Q1036357}} on the {{jalink|Odakyu Enoshima Line|Q1063924}}, {{jalink|Sotetsu Izumino Line|Q846367}}, or {{jalink|Yokohama Municipal Subway Blue Line|Q841360}}: ** {{jalink|Kanagawa Chūō Kōtsū|Q10854960|Kanachu}} Bus ({{jalink|Shō19|Q11588892}}): For Ayase Depot via Keio University and Miyahara; get off at “Utsumochi Shrine Entrance,” 3-minute walk. ** Kanachu Bus ({{jalink|Shō25|Q11588892}}): Twin Liner Express for Keio University; get off at “Keio Junior and Senior High School,” 10-minute walk. * From the north exit of {{jalink|Tsujidō Station|Q1049779}} on the {{jalink|East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|JR}} line: ** Kanachu Bus ({{jalink|Tsuji34|Q11588892}}): For Keio University; get off at “Keio University,” 15-minute walk. === Notes === There are often no staff at the {{jalink|shrine office|Q845945}}, and telephone inquiries may not be possible. If you wish to receive a {{jalink|goshuin|Q7504772}}, it is more reliable to visit during festivals or New Year when events are being held. There are also {{jalink|toilets}} installed within the shrine grounds. === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Further Reading === * Utsumochi Shrine “Shrine Bulletin Board,” Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office “Utsumochi Shrine” page, etc. === Related Articles === {{Wikicommons|Category:Utsumochi-jinja}} * {{jalink|List of Engishiki shrines in Sagami Province|Q11582137}} * {{jalink|List of shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture|Q11588941}} === External Links === * https://utsumochi-jinja.com/ Utsumochi Shrine Official Website * http://www.kanagawa-jinja.or.jp/search_dtl.php4?jid=573&cd=1206054&scd=&npg=0 Utsumochi Shrine (Kanagawa Prefecture {{jalink|Shrine Office|Q11590703}}) {{shinto-stub}} {{Shinto}} {{DEFAULTSORT:utsumochi shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Utsumochi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Utsumochi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Sagami Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:藤沢市の神社]] [[Category:相模国の式内社]] [[Category:神奈川県の旧郷社]] [[Category:kkkkkkkkkkkkkk]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qq]]

category|Category:Utsumochi-jinja}} [[JA:宇都母知神社]] {{wikidata link|Q17217329}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Utsumochi Shrine |Image = [[File:Utsumochi-jinjya 1.JPG|270px]]
Torii gate and worship hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |Location = {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513}}{{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661}} 3012 Uchimoto |ISO = JP-14 |Latitude degrees = 35 | Latitude minutes = 23 | Latitude seconds = 31.3 | N/S = N |Longitude degrees = 139 | Longitude minutes = 25 | Longitude seconds = 17.7 | E/W = E |Enshrined deities = {{ill||lt=Amaterasu Omikami|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill||lt=Wakumusubi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill||lt=Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} |Shrine status = {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} (minor), {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} ({{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine status}}) |Established = Unknown |Main hall style = {{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545}} |Other name = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=September 15|Q2846|WD=Q2846}} |Map = Japan Kanagawa#Japan }} '''Utsumochi Shrine''' (うつもちじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Uchimoto, {{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661}}, {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513}}. It is regarded as one of the shrines (minor shrine) listed among the thirteen {{ill|lt=Engishiki shrine list of Sagami Province|Q11582137|WD=Q11582137|lt=Engishiki shrines}} of {{ill|lt=Sagami Province|Q1053732|WD=Q1053732}} recorded in the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}'' of {{ill|lt=Engi (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Engi}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}).{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1273537/73?tocOpened=1 |title=''Engishiki Vol. 2 (Volumes 8, 9, 10)'', Nihon Koten Zenshū Kankōkai, Showa 4. Frame 73. ({{ill|lt=National Diet Library Digital Collections|Q117959850|WD=Q117959850}}) |publisher= |accessdate=2018-09-03 }} === Enshrined deities === * {{ill||lt=Amaterasu Omikami|UNKNOWN}} * {{ill||lt=Wakumusubi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN}} * {{ill||lt=Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} === Origin and History === The exact date of the shrine’s founding is unknown, but there is a record that solemn {{ill||lt=rituals|UNKNOWN}} were conducted in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235|WD=Q357235}} (according to the {{ill|lt=Kōki era|Q1209728|WD=Q1209728|lt=Imperial Year}} 1119Stone monument within the shrine grounds{{Refnest|group="Note"|The Utsumochi Shrine page (Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office website, viewed September 20, 2017) states {{ill|lt=495|Q30806|WD=Q30806}}.}}) (''Nihon Sōkoku {{ill|lt=Fudoki|Q1194534|WD=Q1194534}}''). Although there are no records of relocation, the same fudoki states that the shrine was located in “Utsumochi-go, Takakura District, Sagami Province.”{{Cite web|Japanese book|author= |url=http://www.kanagawa-jinja.or.jp/search_dtl.php4?jid=573&cd=1206054&scd=&npg=0 |title=Utsumochi Shrine |date=|publisher=Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office |accessdate=2017-09-20 }} In the ''Engishiki Jinmyōchō'', it is recorded as “Utsumochi Shrine, one of the six shrines in {{ill|lt=Kōza District|Q1202776|WD=Q1202776}} among the thirteen shrines of Sagami Province (shikinaisha).” In {{ill|lt=Tengyō|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340}} 2 ({{ill|lt=939|Q23422|WD=Q23422}}), the spirit of Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto was transferred from {{ill|lt=Yamato Province|Q907495|WD=Q907495}}{{ill|lt=Hatsuse|Q11396531|WD=Q11396531|lt=Hase}} and enshrined together in the {{ill|lt=aiden|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883}}. In {{ill|lt=Shōō|Q1327371|WD=Q1327371}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1290|Q5552|WD=Q5552}}), {{ill|lt=Hōjō Sadatoki|Q3136681|WD=Q3136681}}, regent ({{ill|lt=shikken|Q1045904|WD=Q1045904}}) of the eighth shogun {{ill|lt=Prince Hisahito|Q715826|WD=Q715826}} of the {{ill|lt=Kamakura shogunate|Q736839|WD=Q736839}}, rebuilt the shrine buildings. Later, in {{ill|lt=Kan’ei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1627|Q6833|WD=Q6833}}), the local lord {{ill||lt=Takagi Mondo Minamoto no Masatsugu|UNKNOWN}} donated 900 tsubo (about 3,000 {{ill|lt=square meters|Q25343|WD=Q25343|lt=m2}}) of shrine land and rebuilt the main hall. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, the shrine was also known as “Daijingū” and “Shinmeigū.” In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}), it was ranked as a village shrine under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}. In {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 12 ({{ill|lt=1923|Q2169|WD=Q2169}}), the shrine buildings (built in {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1858|Q7684|WD=Q7684}})) were completely destroyed, including the main hall, due to the {{ill|lt=Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341|WD=Q3742341}}. However, they were fully restored three years later in Taishō 15 ({{ill|lt=1926|Q5259|WD=Q5259}}) September, and remain as they are today. The temple bell at the {{ill|lt=bell tower|Q1363585|WD=Q1363585}} located just across the road west of the {{ill|lt=precincts|Q11429002|WD=Q11429002}} was offered in the spring of {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 20 ({{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240}}) for material collection during the {{ill|lt=Pacific War|Q184425|WD=Q184425}}, and was not installed for some time, but was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1975|Q2479|WD=Q2479}}. The entire shrine grounds were designated as a scenic forest, and on March 15, {{ill|lt=1974|Q2478|WD=Q2478}}, it was designated as a {{ill|lt=Natural Environment Conservation Area|Q11612866|WD=Q11612866}}.{{cite web|publisher=Kanagawa Prefecture|url=https://www.pref.kanagawa.jp/docs/t4i/cnt/f10578/p34320.html|title=Designation status of Kanagawa Prefecture Natural Environment Conservation Areas|accessdate=2024-12-05}} === Auxiliary shrines === Within the precincts, in addition to the main shrine, there are the following {{ill||lt=auxiliary shrines|UNKNOWN}}: * {{ill|lt=Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247|WD=Q48532247}} * {{ill|lt=Inari Shrine|Q514480|WD=Q514480}} === Festivals === * {{ill|lt=January 1|Q2150|WD=Q2150}} - {{ill|lt=Saitan-sai|Q11546824|WD=Q11546824}} * {{ill|lt=September 15|Q2846|WD=Q2846}} - '''{{ill|lt=Reitaisai|Q11546824|WD=Q11546824}}''' * The Sunday before {{ill|lt=November 15|Q2982|WD=Q2982}} - {{ill|lt=Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387|WD=Q1072387}} Festival * Last Sunday of November - {{ill|lt=Niiname-sai|Q11501518|WD=Q11501518}} ({{ill|lt=Harvest festival|Q2304773|WD=Q2304773}}) * {{ill|lt=December 30|Q2901|WD=Q2901}} - {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910}} === Reservoir === At 2009 Ōdaira, Uchimoto, there is a shrine-owned {{ill|lt=reservoir|Q112083751|WD=Q112083751}} (about 150 m2). === Origin of the Place Name === One theory regarding the origin of the local place name (Uchimoto) is that it derived from “Uzumochi” (宇豆毛遅) of Utsumochi Shrine, which became corrupted into “Uchimodori.” In {{ill|lt=archaic language|Q11411592|WD=Q11411592}}, both “utsu” and “mochi” are said to mean “small {{ill|lt=basin|Q813672|WD=Q813672}},” and it is believed that the area was first called “Utsumochi” due to its topography, which later changed into “Uchimodori.” Another theory holds that when the swordsmith {{ill|lt=Masamune|Q708935|WD=Q708935|lt=Gorō Masamune}} from {{ill||lt=Ebina|UNKNOWN}} was traveling to {{ill|lt=Kamakura|Q10939227|WD=Q10939227}}, he rested in this area and, dissatisfied with the sword he was about to present, “returned home to reforge it,” which became the origin of the place name.{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=http://www.city.fujisawa.kanagawa.jp/gosho-c/data15331.shtml |title=History of Goshomi: Origin of Place Names |accessdate=August 29, 2013 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140821214356/http://www.city.fujisawa.kanagawa.jp/gosho-c/data15331.shtml |archivedate=August 21, 2014 }} - {{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661}}[Story of Uchimoto] (Fujisawa City Machizukuri Association) === Access === * From the west exit of {{ill|lt=Shōnandai Station|Q1036357|WD=Q1036357}} on the {{ill|lt=Odakyu Enoshima Line|Q1063924|WD=Q1063924}}, {{ill|lt=Sotetsu Izumino Line|Q846367|WD=Q846367}}, or {{ill|lt=Yokohama Municipal Subway Blue Line|Q841360|WD=Q841360}}: ** {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Chūō Kōtsū|Q10854960|WD=Q10854960|lt=Kanachu}} Bus ({{ill|lt=Shō19|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892}}): For Ayase Depot via Keio University and Miyahara; get off at “Utsumochi Shrine Entrance,” 3-minute walk. ** Kanachu Bus ({{ill|lt=Shō25|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892}}): Twin Liner Express for Keio University; get off at “Keio Junior and Senior High School,” 10-minute walk. * From the north exit of {{ill|lt=Tsujidō Station|Q1049779|WD=Q1049779}} on the {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR}} line: ** Kanachu Bus ({{ill|lt=Tsuji34|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892}}): For Keio University; get off at “Keio University,” 15-minute walk. === Notes === There are often no staff at the {{ill|lt=shrine office|Q845945|WD=Q845945}}, and telephone inquiries may not be possible. If you wish to receive a {{ill|lt=goshuin|Q7504772|WD=Q7504772}}, it is more reliable to visit during festivals or New Year when events are being held. There are also {{ill||lt=toilets|UNKNOWN}} installed within the shrine grounds. === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Further Reading === * Utsumochi Shrine “Shrine Bulletin Board,” Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office “Utsumochi Shrine” page, etc. === Related Articles === {{Wikicommons|Category:Utsumochi-jinja}} * {{ill|lt=List of Engishiki shrines in Sagami Province|Q11582137|WD=Q11582137}} * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture|Q11588941|WD=Q11588941}} === External Links === * https://utsumochi-jinja.com/ Utsumochi Shrine Official Website * http://www.kanagawa-jinja.or.jp/search_dtl.php4?jid=573&cd=1206054&scd=&npg=0 Utsumochi Shrine (Kanagawa Prefecture {{ill|lt=Shrine Office|Q11590703|WD=Q11590703}}) {{shinto-stub}} {{Shinto}} {{DEFAULTSORT:utsumochi shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Utsumochi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Utsumochi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Sagami Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:藤沢市の神社]] [[Category:相模国の式内社]] [[Category:神奈川県の旧郷社]] [[Category:kkkkkkkkkkkkkk]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qq]]

Torii gate and worship hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |Location = {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513}}{{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661}} 3012 Uchimoto |ISO = JP-14 |Latitude degrees = 35 | Latitude minutes = 23 | Latitude seconds = 31.3 | N/S = N |Longitude degrees = 139 | Longitude minutes = 25 | Longitude seconds = 17.7 | E/W = E |Enshrined deities = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} |Shrine status = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} (minor), {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} ({{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine status}}) |Established = Unknown |Main hall style = {{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545}} |Other name = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=September 15|Q2846|WD=Q2846}} |Map = Japan Kanagawa#Japan }} '''Utsumochi Shrine''' (うつもちじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Uchimoto, {{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661}}, {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513}}. It is regarded as one of the shrines (minor shrine) listed among the thirteen {{ill|lt=Engishiki shrine list of Sagami Province|Q11582137|WD=Q11582137|lt=Engishiki shrines}} of {{ill|lt=Sagami Province|Q1053732|WD=Q1053732}} recorded in the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}'' of {{ill|lt=Engi (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Engi}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}).{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1273537/73?tocOpened=1 |title=''Engishiki Vol. 2 (Volumes 8, 9, 10)'', Nihon Koten Zenshū Kankōkai, Showa 4. Frame 73. ({{ill|lt=National Diet Library Digital Collections|Q117959850|WD=Q117959850}}) |publisher= |accessdate=2018-09-03 }} === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} === Origin and History === The exact date of the shrine’s founding is unknown, but there is a record that solemn {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=rituals|UNKNOWN}} were conducted in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235|WD=Q357235}} (according to the {{ill|lt=Kōki era|Q1209728|WD=Q1209728|lt=Imperial Year}} 1119Stone monument within the shrine grounds{{Refnest|group="Note"|The Utsumochi Shrine page (Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office website, viewed September 20, 2017) states {{ill|lt=495|Q30806|WD=Q30806}}.}}) (''Nihon Sōkoku {{ill|lt=Fudoki|Q1194534|WD=Q1194534}}''). Although there are no records of relocation, the same fudoki states that the shrine was located in “Utsumochi-go, Takakura District, Sagami Province.”{{Cite web|Japanese book|author= |url=http://www.kanagawa-jinja.or.jp/search_dtl.php4?jid=573&cd=1206054&scd=&npg=0 |title=Utsumochi Shrine |date=|publisher=Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office |accessdate=2017-09-20 }} In the ''Engishiki Jinmyōchō'', it is recorded as “Utsumochi Shrine, one of the six shrines in {{ill|lt=Kōza District|Q1202776|WD=Q1202776}} among the thirteen shrines of Sagami Province (shikinaisha).” In {{ill|lt=Tengyō|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340}} 2 ({{ill|lt=939|Q23422|WD=Q23422}}), the spirit of Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto was transferred from {{ill|lt=Yamato Province|Q907495|WD=Q907495}}{{ill|lt=Hatsuse|Q11396531|WD=Q11396531|lt=Hase}} and enshrined together in the {{ill|lt=aiden|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883}}. In {{ill|lt=Shōō|Q1327371|WD=Q1327371}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1290|Q5552|WD=Q5552}}), {{ill|lt=Hōjō Sadatoki|Q3136681|WD=Q3136681}}, regent ({{ill|lt=shikken|Q1045904|WD=Q1045904}}) of the eighth shogun {{ill|lt=Prince Hisahito|Q715826|WD=Q715826}} of the {{ill|lt=Kamakura shogunate|Q736839|WD=Q736839}}, rebuilt the shrine buildings. Later, in {{ill|lt=Kan’ei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1627|Q6833|WD=Q6833}}), the local lord {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takagi Mondo Minamoto no Masatsugu|UNKNOWN}} donated 900 tsubo (about 3,000 {{ill|lt=square meters|Q25343|WD=Q25343|lt=m2}}) of shrine land and rebuilt the main hall. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, the shrine was also known as “Daijingū” and “Shinmeigū.” In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}), it was ranked as a village shrine under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}. In {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 12 ({{ill|lt=1923|Q2169|WD=Q2169}}), the shrine buildings (built in {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1858|Q7684|WD=Q7684}})) were completely destroyed, including the main hall, due to the {{ill|lt=Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341|WD=Q3742341}}. However, they were fully restored three years later in Taishō 15 ({{ill|lt=1926|Q5259|WD=Q5259}}) September, and remain as they are today. The temple bell at the {{ill|lt=bell tower|Q1363585|WD=Q1363585}} located just across the road west of the {{ill|lt=precincts|Q11429002|WD=Q11429002}} was offered in the spring of {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 20 ({{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240}}) for material collection during the {{ill|lt=Pacific War|Q184425|WD=Q184425}}, and was not installed for some time, but was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1975|Q2479|WD=Q2479}}. The entire shrine grounds were designated as a scenic forest, and on March 15, {{ill|lt=1974|Q2478|WD=Q2478}}, it was designated as a {{ill|lt=Natural Environment Conservation Area|Q11612866|WD=Q11612866}}.{{cite web|publisher=Kanagawa Prefecture|url=https://www.pref.kanagawa.jp/docs/t4i/cnt/f10578/p34320.html|title=Designation status of Kanagawa Prefecture Natural Environment Conservation Areas|accessdate=2024-12-05}} === Auxiliary shrines === Within the precincts, in addition to the main shrine, there are the following {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=auxiliary shrines|UNKNOWN}}: * {{ill|lt=Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247|WD=Q48532247}} * {{ill|lt=Inari Shrine|Q514480|WD=Q514480}} === Festivals === * {{ill|lt=January 1|Q2150|WD=Q2150}} - {{ill|lt=Saitan-sai|Q11546824|WD=Q11546824}} * {{ill|lt=September 15|Q2846|WD=Q2846}} - '''{{ill|lt=Reitaisai|Q11546824|WD=Q11546824}}''' * The Sunday before {{ill|lt=November 15|Q2982|WD=Q2982}} - {{ill|lt=Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387|WD=Q1072387}} Festival * Last Sunday of November - {{ill|lt=Niiname-sai|Q11501518|WD=Q11501518}} ({{ill|lt=Harvest festival|Q2304773|WD=Q2304773}}) * {{ill|lt=December 30|Q2901|WD=Q2901}} - {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910}} === Reservoir === At 2009 Ōdaira, Uchimoto, there is a shrine-owned {{ill|lt=reservoir|Q112083751|WD=Q112083751}} (about 150 m2). === Origin of the Place Name === One theory regarding the origin of the local place name (Uchimoto) is that it derived from “Uzumochi” (宇豆毛遅) of Utsumochi Shrine, which became corrupted into “Uchimodori.” In {{ill|lt=archaic language|Q11411592|WD=Q11411592}}, both “utsu” and “mochi” are said to mean “small {{ill|lt=basin|Q813672|WD=Q813672}},” and it is believed that the area was first called “Utsumochi” due to its topography, which later changed into “Uchimodori.” Another theory holds that when the swordsmith {{ill|lt=Masamune|Q708935|WD=Q708935|lt=Gorō Masamune}} from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ebina|UNKNOWN}} was traveling to {{ill|lt=Kamakura|Q10939227|WD=Q10939227}}, he rested in this area and, dissatisfied with the sword he was about to present, “returned home to reforge it,” which became the origin of the place name.{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=http://www.city.fujisawa.kanagawa.jp/gosho-c/data15331.shtml |title=History of Goshomi: Origin of Place Names |accessdate=August 29, 2013 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140821214356/http://www.city.fujisawa.kanagawa.jp/gosho-c/data15331.shtml |archivedate=August 21, 2014 }} - {{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661}}[Story of Uchimoto] (Fujisawa City Machizukuri Association) === Access === * From the west exit of {{ill|lt=Shōnandai Station|Q1036357|WD=Q1036357}} on the {{ill|lt=Odakyu Enoshima Line|Q1063924|WD=Q1063924}}, {{ill|lt=Sotetsu Izumino Line|Q846367|WD=Q846367}}, or {{ill|lt=Yokohama Municipal Subway Blue Line|Q841360|WD=Q841360}}: ** {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Chūō Kōtsū|Q10854960|WD=Q10854960|lt=Kanachu}} Bus ({{ill|lt=Shō19|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892}}): For Ayase Depot via Keio University and Miyahara; get off at “Utsumochi Shrine Entrance,” 3-minute walk. ** Kanachu Bus ({{ill|lt=Shō25|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892}}): Twin Liner Express for Keio University; get off at “Keio Junior and Senior High School,” 10-minute walk. * From the north exit of {{ill|lt=Tsujidō Station|Q1049779|WD=Q1049779}} on the {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR}} line: ** Kanachu Bus ({{ill|lt=Tsuji34|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892}}): For Keio University; get off at “Keio University,” 15-minute walk. === Notes === There are often no staff at the {{ill|lt=shrine office|Q845945|WD=Q845945}}, and telephone inquiries may not be possible. If you wish to receive a {{ill|lt=goshuin|Q7504772|WD=Q7504772}}, it is more reliable to visit during festivals or New Year when events are being held. There are also {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=toilets|UNKNOWN}} installed within the shrine grounds. === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Further Reading === * Utsumochi Shrine “Shrine Bulletin Board,” Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office “Utsumochi Shrine” page, etc. === Related Articles === {{Wikicommons|Category:Utsumochi-jinja}} * {{ill|lt=List of Engishiki shrines in Sagami Province|Q11582137|WD=Q11582137}} * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture|Q11588941|WD=Q11588941}} === External Links === * https://utsumochi-jinja.com/ Utsumochi Shrine Official Website * http://www.kanagawa-jinja.or.jp/search_dtl.php4?jid=573&cd=1206054&scd=&npg=0 Utsumochi Shrine (Kanagawa Prefecture {{ill|lt=Shrine Office|Q11590703|WD=Q11590703}}) {{shinto-stub}} {{Shinto}} {{DEFAULTSORT:utsumochi shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Utsumochi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Utsumochi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Sagami Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:藤沢市の神社]] [[Category:相模国の式内社]] [[Category:神奈川県の旧郷社]] [[Category:kkkkkkkkkkkkkk]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qq]]

category|Category:Utsumochi-jinja}} [[JA:宇都母知神社]] {{wikidata link|Q17217329}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Utsumochi Shrine |Image = [[File:Utsumochi-jinjya 1.JPG|270px]]
Torii gate and worship hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |Location = {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513}}{{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661}} 3012 Uchimoto |ISO = JP-14 |Latitude degrees = 35 | Latitude minutes = 23 | Latitude seconds = 31.3 | N/S = N |Longitude degrees = 139 | Longitude minutes = 25 | Longitude seconds = 17.7 | E/W = E |Enshrined deities = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} |Shrine status = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} (minor), {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} ({{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine status}}) |Established = Unknown |Main hall style = {{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545}} |Other name = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=September 15|Q2846|WD=Q2846}} |Map = Japan Kanagawa#Japan }} '''Utsumochi Shrine''' (うつもちじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Uchimoto, {{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661}}, {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513}}. It is regarded as one of the shrines (minor shrine) listed among the thirteen {{ill|lt=Engishiki shrine list of Sagami Province|Q11582137|WD=Q11582137|lt=Engishiki shrines}} of {{ill|lt=Sagami Province|Q1053732|WD=Q1053732}} recorded in the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}'' of {{ill|lt=Engi (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Engi}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}).{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1273537/73?tocOpened=1 |title=''Engishiki Vol. 2 (Volumes 8, 9, 10)'', Nihon Koten Zenshū Kankōkai, Showa 4. Frame 73. ({{ill|lt=National Diet Library Digital Collections|Q117959850|WD=Q117959850}}) |publisher= |accessdate=2018-09-03 }} === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} === Origin and History === The exact date of the shrine’s founding is unknown, but there is a record that solemn {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=rituals|1=UNKNOWN}} were conducted in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235|WD=Q357235}} (according to the {{ill|lt=Kōki era|Q1209728|WD=Q1209728|lt=Imperial Year}} 1119Stone monument within the shrine grounds{{Refnest|group="Note"|The Utsumochi Shrine page (Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office website, viewed September 20, 2017) states {{ill|lt=495|Q30806|WD=Q30806}}.}}) (''Nihon Sōkoku {{ill|lt=Fudoki|Q1194534|WD=Q1194534}}''). Although there are no records of relocation, the same fudoki states that the shrine was located in “Utsumochi-go, Takakura District, Sagami Province.”{{Cite web|Japanese book|author= |url=http://www.kanagawa-jinja.or.jp/search_dtl.php4?jid=573&cd=1206054&scd=&npg=0 |title=Utsumochi Shrine |date=|publisher=Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office |accessdate=2017-09-20 }} In the ''Engishiki Jinmyōchō'', it is recorded as “Utsumochi Shrine, one of the six shrines in {{ill|lt=Kōza District|Q1202776|WD=Q1202776}} among the thirteen shrines of Sagami Province (shikinaisha).” In {{ill|lt=Tengyō|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340}} 2 ({{ill|lt=939|Q23422|WD=Q23422}}), the spirit of Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto was transferred from {{ill|lt=Yamato Province|Q907495|WD=Q907495}}{{ill|lt=Hatsuse|Q11396531|WD=Q11396531|lt=Hase}} and enshrined together in the {{ill|lt=aiden|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883}}. In {{ill|lt=Shōō|Q1327371|WD=Q1327371}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1290|Q5552|WD=Q5552}}), {{ill|lt=Hōjō Sadatoki|Q3136681|WD=Q3136681}}, regent ({{ill|lt=shikken|Q1045904|WD=Q1045904}}) of the eighth shogun {{ill|lt=Prince Hisahito|Q715826|WD=Q715826}} of the {{ill|lt=Kamakura shogunate|Q736839|WD=Q736839}}, rebuilt the shrine buildings. Later, in {{ill|lt=Kan’ei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1627|Q6833|WD=Q6833}}), the local lord {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takagi Mondo Minamoto no Masatsugu|1=UNKNOWN}} donated 900 tsubo (about 3,000 {{ill|lt=square meters|Q25343|WD=Q25343|lt=m2}}) of shrine land and rebuilt the main hall. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, the shrine was also known as “Daijingū” and “Shinmeigū.” In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}), it was ranked as a village shrine under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}. In {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 12 ({{ill|lt=1923|Q2169|WD=Q2169}}), the shrine buildings (built in {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1858|Q7684|WD=Q7684}})) were completely destroyed, including the main hall, due to the {{ill|lt=Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341|WD=Q3742341}}. However, they were fully restored three years later in Taishō 15 ({{ill|lt=1926|Q5259|WD=Q5259}}) September, and remain as they are today. The temple bell at the {{ill|lt=bell tower|Q1363585|WD=Q1363585}} located just across the road west of the {{ill|lt=precincts|Q11429002|WD=Q11429002}} was offered in the spring of {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 20 ({{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240}}) for material collection during the {{ill|lt=Pacific War|Q184425|WD=Q184425}}, and was not installed for some time, but was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1975|Q2479|WD=Q2479}}. The entire shrine grounds were designated as a scenic forest, and on March 15, {{ill|lt=1974|Q2478|WD=Q2478}}, it was designated as a {{ill|lt=Natural Environment Conservation Area|Q11612866|WD=Q11612866}}.{{cite web|publisher=Kanagawa Prefecture|url=https://www.pref.kanagawa.jp/docs/t4i/cnt/f10578/p34320.html|title=Designation status of Kanagawa Prefecture Natural Environment Conservation Areas|accessdate=2024-12-05}} === Auxiliary shrines === Within the precincts, in addition to the main shrine, there are the following {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=auxiliary shrines|1=UNKNOWN}}: * {{ill|lt=Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247|WD=Q48532247}} * {{ill|lt=Inari Shrine|Q514480|WD=Q514480}} === Festivals === * {{ill|lt=January 1|Q2150|WD=Q2150}} - {{ill|lt=Saitan-sai|Q11546824|WD=Q11546824}} * {{ill|lt=September 15|Q2846|WD=Q2846}} - '''{{ill|lt=Reitaisai|Q11546824|WD=Q11546824}}''' * The Sunday before {{ill|lt=November 15|Q2982|WD=Q2982}} - {{ill|lt=Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387|WD=Q1072387}} Festival * Last Sunday of November - {{ill|lt=Niiname-sai|Q11501518|WD=Q11501518}} ({{ill|lt=Harvest festival|Q2304773|WD=Q2304773}}) * {{ill|lt=December 30|Q2901|WD=Q2901}} - {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910}} === Reservoir === At 2009 Ōdaira, Uchimoto, there is a shrine-owned {{ill|lt=reservoir|Q112083751|WD=Q112083751}} (about 150 m2). === Origin of the Place Name === One theory regarding the origin of the local place name (Uchimoto) is that it derived from “Uzumochi” (宇豆毛遅) of Utsumochi Shrine, which became corrupted into “Uchimodori.” In {{ill|lt=archaic language|Q11411592|WD=Q11411592}}, both “utsu” and “mochi” are said to mean “small {{ill|lt=basin|Q813672|WD=Q813672}},” and it is believed that the area was first called “Utsumochi” due to its topography, which later changed into “Uchimodori.” Another theory holds that when the swordsmith {{ill|lt=Masamune|Q708935|WD=Q708935|lt=Gorō Masamune}} from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ebina|1=UNKNOWN}} was traveling to {{ill|lt=Kamakura|Q10939227|WD=Q10939227}}, he rested in this area and, dissatisfied with the sword he was about to present, “returned home to reforge it,” which became the origin of the place name.{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=http://www.city.fujisawa.kanagawa.jp/gosho-c/data15331.shtml |title=History of Goshomi: Origin of Place Names |accessdate=August 29, 2013 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140821214356/http://www.city.fujisawa.kanagawa.jp/gosho-c/data15331.shtml |archivedate=August 21, 2014 }} - {{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661}}[Story of Uchimoto] (Fujisawa City Machizukuri Association) === Access === * From the west exit of {{ill|lt=Shōnandai Station|Q1036357|WD=Q1036357}} on the {{ill|lt=Odakyu Enoshima Line|Q1063924|WD=Q1063924}}, {{ill|lt=Sotetsu Izumino Line|Q846367|WD=Q846367}}, or {{ill|lt=Yokohama Municipal Subway Blue Line|Q841360|WD=Q841360}}: ** {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Chūō Kōtsū|Q10854960|WD=Q10854960|lt=Kanachu}} Bus ({{ill|lt=Shō19|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892}}): For Ayase Depot via Keio University and Miyahara; get off at “Utsumochi Shrine Entrance,” 3-minute walk. ** Kanachu Bus ({{ill|lt=Shō25|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892}}): Twin Liner Express for Keio University; get off at “Keio Junior and Senior High School,” 10-minute walk. * From the north exit of {{ill|lt=Tsujidō Station|Q1049779|WD=Q1049779}} on the {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR}} line: ** Kanachu Bus ({{ill|lt=Tsuji34|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892}}): For Keio University; get off at “Keio University,” 15-minute walk. === Notes === There are often no staff at the {{ill|lt=shrine office|Q845945|WD=Q845945}}, and telephone inquiries may not be possible. If you wish to receive a {{ill|lt=goshuin|Q7504772|WD=Q7504772}}, it is more reliable to visit during festivals or New Year when events are being held. There are also {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=toilets|1=UNKNOWN}} installed within the shrine grounds. === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Further Reading === * Utsumochi Shrine “Shrine Bulletin Board,” Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office “Utsumochi Shrine” page, etc. === Related Articles === {{Wikicommons|Category:Utsumochi-jinja}} * {{ill|lt=List of Engishiki shrines in Sagami Province|Q11582137|WD=Q11582137}} * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture|Q11588941|WD=Q11588941}} === External Links === * https://utsumochi-jinja.com/ Utsumochi Shrine Official Website * http://www.kanagawa-jinja.or.jp/search_dtl.php4?jid=573&cd=1206054&scd=&npg=0 Utsumochi Shrine (Kanagawa Prefecture {{ill|lt=Shrine Office|Q11590703|WD=Q11590703}}) {{shinto-stub}} {{Shinto}} {{DEFAULTSORT:utsumochi shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Utsumochi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Utsumochi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Sagami Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:藤沢市の神社]] [[Category:相模国の式内社]] [[Category:神奈川県の旧郷社]] [[Category:kkkkkkkkkkkkkk]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qq]]

category|Category:Utsumochi-jinja}} [[JA:宇都母知神社]] {{wikidata link|Q17217329}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Utsumochi Shrine |Image = [[File:Utsumochi-jinjya 1.JPG|270px]]
Torii gate and worship hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |Location = {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513}}{{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661}} 3012 Uchimoto |ISO = JP-14 |Latitude degrees = 35 | Latitude minutes = 23 | Latitude seconds = 31.3 | N/S = N |Longitude degrees = 139 | Longitude minutes = 25 | Longitude seconds = 17.7 | E/W = E |Enshrined deities = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} |Shrine status = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (minor), {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} ({{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine status}}) |Established = Unknown |Main hall style = {{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545}} |Other name = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=September 15|Q2846|WD=Q2846}} |Map = Japan Kanagawa#Japan }} '''Utsumochi Shrine''' (うつもちじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Uchimoto, {{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661}}, {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513}}. It is regarded as one of the shrines (minor shrine) listed among the thirteen {{ill|lt=Engishiki shrine list of Sagami Province|Q11582137|WD=Q11582137|lt=Engishiki shrines}} of {{ill|lt=Sagami Province|Q1053732|WD=Q1053732}} recorded in the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}'' of {{ill|lt=Engi (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Engi}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}).{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1273537/73?tocOpened=1 |title=''Engishiki Vol. 2 (Volumes 8, 9, 10)'', Nihon Koten Zenshū Kankōkai, Showa 4. Frame 73. ({{ill|lt=National Diet Library Digital Collections|Q117959850|WD=Q117959850}}) |publisher= |accessdate=2018-09-03 }} === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} === Origin and History === The exact date of the shrine’s founding is unknown, but there is a record that solemn {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=rituals|1=UNKNOWN}} were conducted in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235|WD=Q357235}} (according to the {{ill|lt=Kōki era|Q1209728|WD=Q1209728|lt=Imperial Year}} 1119Stone monument within the shrine grounds{{Refnest|group="Note"|The Utsumochi Shrine page (Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office website, viewed September 20, 2017) states {{ill|lt=495|Q30806|WD=Q30806}}.}}) (''Nihon Sōkoku {{ill|lt=Fudoki|Q1194534|WD=Q1194534}}''). Although there are no records of relocation, the same fudoki states that the shrine was located in “Utsumochi-go, Takakura District, Sagami Province.”{{Cite web|Japanese book|author= |url=http://www.kanagawa-jinja.or.jp/search_dtl.php4?jid=573&cd=1206054&scd=&npg=0 |title=Utsumochi Shrine |date=|publisher=Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office |accessdate=2017-09-20 }} In the ''Engishiki Jinmyōchō'', it is recorded as “Utsumochi Shrine, one of the six shrines in {{ill|lt=Kōza District|Q1202776|WD=Q1202776}} among the thirteen shrines of Sagami Province (shikinaisha).” In {{ill|lt=Tengyō|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340}} 2 ({{ill|lt=939|Q23422|WD=Q23422}}), the spirit of Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto was transferred from {{ill|lt=Yamato Province|Q907495|WD=Q907495}}{{ill|lt=Hatsuse|Q11396531|WD=Q11396531|lt=Hase}} and enshrined together in the {{ill|lt=aiden|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883}}. In {{ill|lt=Shōō|Q1327371|WD=Q1327371}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1290|Q5552|WD=Q5552}}), {{ill|lt=Hōjō Sadatoki|Q3136681|WD=Q3136681}}, regent ({{ill|lt=shikken|Q1045904|WD=Q1045904}}) of the eighth shogun {{ill|lt=Prince Hisahito|Q715826|WD=Q715826}} of the {{ill|lt=Kamakura shogunate|Q736839|WD=Q736839}}, rebuilt the shrine buildings. Later, in {{ill|lt=Kan’ei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1627|Q6833|WD=Q6833}}), the local lord {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takagi Mondo Minamoto no Masatsugu|1=UNKNOWN}} donated 900 tsubo (about 3,000 {{ill|lt=square meters|Q25343|WD=Q25343|lt=m2}}) of shrine land and rebuilt the main hall. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, the shrine was also known as “Daijingū” and “Shinmeigū.” In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}), it was ranked as a village shrine under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}. In {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 12 ({{ill|lt=1923|Q2169|WD=Q2169}}), the shrine buildings (built in {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1858|Q7684|WD=Q7684}})) were completely destroyed, including the main hall, due to the {{ill|lt=Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341|WD=Q3742341}}. However, they were fully restored three years later in Taishō 15 ({{ill|lt=1926|Q5259|WD=Q5259}}) September, and remain as they are today. The temple bell at the {{ill|lt=bell tower|Q1363585|WD=Q1363585}} located just across the road west of the {{ill|lt=precincts|Q11429002|WD=Q11429002}} was offered in the spring of {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 20 ({{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240}}) for material collection during the {{ill|lt=Pacific War|Q184425|WD=Q184425}}, and was not installed for some time, but was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1975|Q2479|WD=Q2479}}. The entire shrine grounds were designated as a scenic forest, and on March 15, {{ill|lt=1974|Q2478|WD=Q2478}}, it was designated as a {{ill|lt=Natural Environment Conservation Area|Q11612866|WD=Q11612866}}.{{cite web|publisher=Kanagawa Prefecture|url=https://www.pref.kanagawa.jp/docs/t4i/cnt/f10578/p34320.html|title=Designation status of Kanagawa Prefecture Natural Environment Conservation Areas|accessdate=2024-12-05}} === Auxiliary shrines === Within the precincts, in addition to the main shrine, there are the following {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=auxiliary shrines|1=UNKNOWN}}: * {{ill|lt=Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247|WD=Q48532247}} * {{ill|lt=Inari Shrine|Q514480|WD=Q514480}} === Festivals === * {{ill|lt=January 1|Q2150|WD=Q2150}} - {{ill|lt=Saitan-sai|Q11546824|WD=Q11546824}} * {{ill|lt=September 15|Q2846|WD=Q2846}} - '''{{ill|lt=Reitaisai|Q11546824|WD=Q11546824}}''' * The Sunday before {{ill|lt=November 15|Q2982|WD=Q2982}} - {{ill|lt=Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387|WD=Q1072387}} Festival * Last Sunday of November - {{ill|lt=Niiname-sai|Q11501518|WD=Q11501518}} ({{ill|lt=Harvest festival|Q2304773|WD=Q2304773}}) * {{ill|lt=December 30|Q2901|WD=Q2901}} - {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910}} === Reservoir === At 2009 Ōdaira, Uchimoto, there is a shrine-owned {{ill|lt=reservoir|Q112083751|WD=Q112083751}} (about 150 m2). === Origin of the Place Name === One theory regarding the origin of the local place name (Uchimoto) is that it derived from “Uzumochi” (宇豆毛遅) of Utsumochi Shrine, which became corrupted into “Uchimodori.” In {{ill|lt=archaic language|Q11411592|WD=Q11411592}}, both “utsu” and “mochi” are said to mean “small {{ill|lt=basin|Q813672|WD=Q813672}},” and it is believed that the area was first called “Utsumochi” due to its topography, which later changed into “Uchimodori.” Another theory holds that when the swordsmith {{ill|lt=Masamune|Q708935|WD=Q708935|lt=Gorō Masamune}} from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ebina|1=UNKNOWN}} was traveling to {{ill|lt=Kamakura|Q10939227|WD=Q10939227}}, he rested in this area and, dissatisfied with the sword he was about to present, “returned home to reforge it,” which became the origin of the place name.{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=http://www.city.fujisawa.kanagawa.jp/gosho-c/data15331.shtml |title=History of Goshomi: Origin of Place Names |accessdate=August 29, 2013 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140821214356/http://www.city.fujisawa.kanagawa.jp/gosho-c/data15331.shtml |archivedate=August 21, 2014 }} - {{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661}}[Story of Uchimoto] (Fujisawa City Machizukuri Association) === Access === * From the west exit of {{ill|lt=Shōnandai Station|Q1036357|WD=Q1036357}} on the {{ill|lt=Odakyu Enoshima Line|Q1063924|WD=Q1063924}}, {{ill|lt=Sotetsu Izumino Line|Q846367|WD=Q846367}}, or {{ill|lt=Yokohama Municipal Subway Blue Line|Q841360|WD=Q841360}}: ** {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Chūō Kōtsū|Q10854960|WD=Q10854960|lt=Kanachu}} Bus ({{ill|lt=Shō19|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892}}): For Ayase Depot via Keio University and Miyahara; get off at “Utsumochi Shrine Entrance,” 3-minute walk. ** Kanachu Bus ({{ill|lt=Shō25|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892}}): Twin Liner Express for Keio University; get off at “Keio Junior and Senior High School,” 10-minute walk. * From the north exit of {{ill|lt=Tsujidō Station|Q1049779|WD=Q1049779}} on the {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR}} line: ** Kanachu Bus ({{ill|lt=Tsuji34|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892}}): For Keio University; get off at “Keio University,” 15-minute walk. === Notes === There are often no staff at the {{ill|lt=shrine office|Q845945|WD=Q845945}}, and telephone inquiries may not be possible. If you wish to receive a {{ill|lt=goshuin|Q7504772|WD=Q7504772}}, it is more reliable to visit during festivals or New Year when events are being held. There are also {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=toilets|1=UNKNOWN}} installed within the shrine grounds. === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Further Reading === * Utsumochi Shrine “Shrine Bulletin Board,” Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office “Utsumochi Shrine” page, etc. === Related Articles === {{Wikicommons|Category:Utsumochi-jinja}} * {{ill|lt=List of Engishiki shrines in Sagami Province|Q11582137|WD=Q11582137}} * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture|Q11588941|WD=Q11588941}} === External Links === * https://utsumochi-jinja.com/ Utsumochi Shrine Official Website * http://www.kanagawa-jinja.or.jp/search_dtl.php4?jid=573&cd=1206054&scd=&npg=0 Utsumochi Shrine (Kanagawa Prefecture {{ill|lt=Shrine Office|Q11590703|WD=Q11590703}}) {{shinto-stub}} {{Shinto}} {{DEFAULTSORT:utsumochi shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Utsumochi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Utsumochi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Sagami Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:藤沢市の神社]] [[Category:相模国の式内社]] [[Category:神奈川県の旧郷社]] [[Category:kkkkkkkkkkkkkk]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qq]]

{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |Location = {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513}}{{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661}} 3012 Uchimoto |ISO = JP-14 |Latitude degrees = 35 | Latitude minutes = 23 | Latitude seconds = 31.3 | N/S = N |Longitude degrees = 139 | Longitude minutes = 25 | Longitude seconds = 17.7 | E/W = E |Enshrined deities = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} |Shrine status = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (minor), {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} ({{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine status}}) |Established = Unknown |Main hall style = {{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545}} |Other name = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=September 15|Q2846|WD=Q2846}} |Map = Japan Kanagawa#Japan }} '''Utsumochi Shrine''' (うつもちじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Uchimoto, {{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661}}, {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513}}. It is regarded as one of the shrines (minor shrine) listed among the thirteen {{ill|lt=Engishiki shrine list of Sagami Province|Q11582137|WD=Q11582137|lt=Engishiki shrines}} of {{ill|lt=Sagami Province|Q1053732|WD=Q1053732}} recorded in the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}'' of {{ill|lt=Engi (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Engi}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}).{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1273537/73?tocOpened=1 |title=''Engishiki Vol. 2 (Volumes 8, 9, 10)'', Nihon Koten Zenshū Kankōkai, Showa 4. Frame 73. ({{ill|lt=National Diet Library Digital Collections|Q117959850|WD=Q117959850}}) |publisher= |accessdate=2018-09-03 }} === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} === Origin and History === The exact date of the shrine’s founding is unknown, but there is a record that solemn {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=rituals|1=UNKNOWN}} were conducted in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235|WD=Q357235}} (according to the {{ill|lt=Kōki era|Q1209728|WD=Q1209728|lt=Imperial Year}} 1119Stone monument within the shrine grounds{{Refnest|group="Note"|The Utsumochi Shrine page (Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office website, viewed September 20, 2017) states {{ill|lt=495|Q30806|WD=Q30806}}.}}) (''Nihon Sōkoku {{ill|lt=Fudoki|Q1194534|WD=Q1194534}}''). Although there are no records of relocation, the same fudoki states that the shrine was located in “Utsumochi-go, Takakura District, Sagami Province.”{{Cite web|Japanese book|author= |url=http://www.kanagawa-jinja.or.jp/search_dtl.php4?jid=573&cd=1206054&scd=&npg=0 |title=Utsumochi Shrine |date=|publisher=Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office |accessdate=2017-09-20 }} In the ''Engishiki Jinmyōchō'', it is recorded as “Utsumochi Shrine, one of the six shrines in {{ill|lt=Kōza District|Q1202776|WD=Q1202776}} among the thirteen shrines of Sagami Province (shikinaisha).” In {{ill|lt=Tengyō|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340}} 2 ({{ill|lt=939|Q23422|WD=Q23422}}), the spirit of Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto was transferred from {{ill|lt=Yamato Province|Q907495|WD=Q907495}}{{ill|lt=Hatsuse|Q11396531|WD=Q11396531|lt=Hase}} and enshrined together in the {{ill|lt=aiden|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883}}. In {{ill|lt=Shōō|Q1327371|WD=Q1327371}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1290|Q5552|WD=Q5552}}), {{ill|lt=Hōjō Sadatoki|Q3136681|WD=Q3136681}}, regent ({{ill|lt=shikken|Q1045904|WD=Q1045904}}) of the eighth shogun {{ill|lt=Prince Hisahito|Q715826|WD=Q715826}} of the {{ill|lt=Kamakura shogunate|Q736839|WD=Q736839}}, rebuilt the shrine buildings. Later, in {{ill|lt=Kan’ei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1627|Q6833|WD=Q6833}}), the local lord {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takagi Mondo Minamoto no Masatsugu|1=UNKNOWN}} donated 900 tsubo (about 3,000 {{ill|lt=square meters|Q25343|WD=Q25343|lt=m2}}) of shrine land and rebuilt the main hall. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, the shrine was also known as “Daijingū” and “Shinmeigū.” In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}), it was ranked as a village shrine under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}. In {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 12 ({{ill|lt=1923|Q2169|WD=Q2169}}), the shrine buildings (built in {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1858|Q7684|WD=Q7684}})) were completely destroyed, including the main hall, due to the {{ill|lt=Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341|WD=Q3742341}}. However, they were fully restored three years later in Taishō 15 ({{ill|lt=1926|Q5259|WD=Q5259}}) September, and remain as they are today. The temple bell at the {{ill|lt=bell tower|Q1363585|WD=Q1363585}} located just across the road west of the {{ill|lt=precincts|Q11429002|WD=Q11429002}} was offered in the spring of {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 20 ({{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240}}) for material collection during the {{ill|lt=Pacific War|Q184425|WD=Q184425}}, and was not installed for some time, but was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1975|Q2479|WD=Q2479}}. The entire shrine grounds were designated as a scenic forest, and on March 15, {{ill|lt=1974|Q2478|WD=Q2478}}, it was designated as a {{ill|lt=Natural Environment Conservation Area|Q11612866|WD=Q11612866}}.{{cite web|publisher=Kanagawa Prefecture|url=https://www.pref.kanagawa.jp/docs/t4i/cnt/f10578/p34320.html|title=Designation status of Kanagawa Prefecture Natural Environment Conservation Areas|accessdate=2024-12-05}} === Auxiliary shrines === Within the precincts, in addition to the main shrine, there are the following {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=auxiliary shrines|1=UNKNOWN}}: * {{ill|lt=Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247|WD=Q48532247}} * {{ill|lt=Inari Shrine|Q514480|WD=Q514480}} === Festivals === * {{ill|lt=January 1|Q2150|WD=Q2150}} - {{ill|lt=Saitan-sai|Q11546824|WD=Q11546824}} * {{ill|lt=September 15|Q2846|WD=Q2846}} - '''{{ill|lt=Reitaisai|Q11546824|WD=Q11546824}}''' * The Sunday before {{ill|lt=November 15|Q2982|WD=Q2982}} - {{ill|lt=Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387|WD=Q1072387}} Festival * Last Sunday of November - {{ill|lt=Niiname-sai|Q11501518|WD=Q11501518}} ({{ill|lt=Harvest festival|Q2304773|WD=Q2304773}}) * {{ill|lt=December 30|Q2901|WD=Q2901}} - {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910}} === Reservoir === At 2009 Ōdaira, Uchimoto, there is a shrine-owned {{ill|lt=reservoir|Q112083751|WD=Q112083751}} (about 150 m2). === Origin of the Place Name === One theory regarding the origin of the local place name (Uchimoto) is that it derived from “Uzumochi” (宇豆毛遅) of Utsumochi Shrine, which became corrupted into “Uchimodori.” In {{ill|lt=archaic language|Q11411592|WD=Q11411592}}, both “utsu” and “mochi” are said to mean “small {{ill|lt=basin|Q813672|WD=Q813672}},” and it is believed that the area was first called “Utsumochi” due to its topography, which later changed into “Uchimodori.” Another theory holds that when the swordsmith {{ill|lt=Masamune|Q708935|WD=Q708935|lt=Gorō Masamune}} from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ebina|1=UNKNOWN}} was traveling to {{ill|lt=Kamakura|Q10939227|WD=Q10939227}}, he rested in this area and, dissatisfied with the sword he was about to present, “returned home to reforge it,” which became the origin of the place name.{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=http://www.city.fujisawa.kanagawa.jp/gosho-c/data15331.shtml |title=History of Goshomi: Origin of Place Names |accessdate=August 29, 2013 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140821214356/http://www.city.fujisawa.kanagawa.jp/gosho-c/data15331.shtml |archivedate=August 21, 2014 }} - {{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661}}[Story of Uchimoto] (Fujisawa City Machizukuri Association) === Access === * From the west exit of {{ill|lt=Shōnandai Station|Q1036357|WD=Q1036357}} on the {{ill|lt=Odakyu Enoshima Line|Q1063924|WD=Q1063924}}, {{ill|lt=Sotetsu Izumino Line|Q846367|WD=Q846367}}, or {{ill|lt=Yokohama Municipal Subway Blue Line|Q841360|WD=Q841360}}: ** {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Chūō Kōtsū|Q10854960|WD=Q10854960|lt=Kanachu}} Bus ({{ill|lt=Shō19|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892}}): For Ayase Depot via Keio University and Miyahara; get off at “Utsumochi Shrine Entrance,” 3-minute walk. ** Kanachu Bus ({{ill|lt=Shō25|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892}}): Twin Liner Express for Keio University; get off at “Keio Junior and Senior High School,” 10-minute walk. * From the north exit of {{ill|lt=Tsujidō Station|Q1049779|WD=Q1049779}} on the {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR}} line: ** Kanachu Bus ({{ill|lt=Tsuji34|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892}}): For Keio University; get off at “Keio University,” 15-minute walk. === Notes === There are often no staff at the {{ill|lt=shrine office|Q845945|WD=Q845945}}, and telephone inquiries may not be possible. If you wish to receive a {{ill|lt=goshuin|Q7504772|WD=Q7504772}}, it is more reliable to visit during festivals or New Year when events are being held. There are also {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=toilets|1=UNKNOWN}} installed within the shrine grounds. === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Further Reading === * Utsumochi Shrine “Shrine Bulletin Board,” Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office “Utsumochi Shrine” page, etc. === Related Articles === {{Wikicommons|Category:Utsumochi-jinja}} * {{ill|lt=List of Engishiki shrines in Sagami Province|Q11582137|WD=Q11582137}} * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture|Q11588941|WD=Q11588941}} === External Links === * https://utsumochi-jinja.com/ Utsumochi Shrine Official Website * http://www.kanagawa-jinja.or.jp/search_dtl.php4?jid=573&cd=1206054&scd=&npg=0 Utsumochi Shrine (Kanagawa Prefecture {{ill|lt=Shrine Office|Q11590703|WD=Q11590703}}) {{shinto-stub}} {{Shinto}} {{DEFAULTSORT:utsumochi shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Utsumochi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Utsumochi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Sagami Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:藤沢市の神社]] [[Category:相模国の式内社]] [[Category:神奈川県の旧郷社]] [[Category:kkkkkkkkkkkkkk]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qq]]
