== Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 4021005000153 == Commons category (P373) == * Utsumochi-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '神奈川県藤沢市打戻3012', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Utsumochi-jinja}} [[JA:宇都母知神社]] {{wikidata link|Q17217329}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Utsumochi Shrine |Image = [[File:Utsumochi-jinjya 1.JPG|270px]]
Torii gate and worship hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |Location = {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661|1=Fujisawa, Kanagawa}} 3012 Uchimoto |ISO = JP-14 |Latitude degrees = 35 | Latitude minutes = 23 | Latitude seconds = 31.3 | N/S = N |Longitude degrees = 139 | Longitude minutes = 25 | Longitude seconds = 17.7 | E/W = E |Enshrined deities = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Kami|1=Wakumusubi}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} |Shrine status = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor), {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} ({{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine status|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}) |Established = Unknown |Main hall style = {{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} |Other name = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=September 15|Q2846|WD=Q2846|1=September 15}} |Map = Japan Kanagawa#Japan }} '''Utsumochi Shrine''' (うつもちじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Uchimoto, {{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661|1=Fujisawa, Kanagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}. It is regarded as one of the shrines (minor shrine) listed among the thirteen {{ill|lt=Engishiki shrine list of Sagami Province|Q11582137|WD=Q11582137|lt=Engishiki shrines|1=List of Shikinaisha in Sagami Province}} of {{ill|lt=Sagami Province|Q1053732|WD=Q1053732|1=Sagami Province}} recorded in the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'' of {{ill|lt=Engi (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Engi|1=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}).{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1273537/73?tocOpened=1 |title=''Engishiki Vol. 2 (Volumes 8, 9, 10)'', Nihon Koten Zenshū Kankōkai, Showa 4. Frame 73. ({{ill|lt=National Diet Library Digital Collections|Q117959850|WD=Q117959850|1=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}) |publisher= |accessdate=2018-09-03 }} === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Kami|1=Wakumusubi}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} === Origin and History === The exact date of the shrine’s founding is unknown, but there is a record that solemn {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=rituals|1=UNKNOWN}} were conducted in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235|WD=Q357235|1=Emperor Yūryaku}} (according to the {{ill|lt=Kōki era|Q1209728|WD=Q1209728|lt=Imperial Year|1=Japanese imperial year}} 1119Stone monument within the shrine grounds{{Refnest|group="Note"|The Utsumochi Shrine page (Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office website, viewed September 20, 2017) states {{ill|lt=495|Q30806|WD=Q30806|1=495}}.}}) (''Nihon Sōkoku {{ill|lt=Fudoki|Q1194534|WD=Q1194534|1=Fudoki}}''). Although there are no records of relocation, the same fudoki states that the shrine was located in “Utsumochi-go, Takakura District, Sagami Province.”{{Cite web|Japanese book|author= |url=http://www.kanagawa-jinja.or.jp/search_dtl.php4?jid=573&cd=1206054&scd=&npg=0 |title=Utsumochi Shrine |date=|publisher=Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office |accessdate=2017-09-20 }} In the ''Engishiki Jinmyōchō'', it is recorded as “Utsumochi Shrine, one of the six shrines in {{ill|lt=Kōza District|Q1202776|WD=Q1202776|1=Kōza District, Kanagawa}} among the thirteen shrines of Sagami Province (shikinaisha).” In {{ill|lt=Tengyō|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340|1=Tengyō}} 2 ({{ill|lt=939|Q23422|WD=Q23422|1=939}}), the spirit of Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto was transferred from {{ill|lt=Yamato Province|Q907495|WD=Q907495|1=Yamato Province}}{{ill|lt=Hatsuse|Q11396531|WD=Q11396531|lt=Hase|1=Hase}} and enshrined together in the {{ill|lt=aiden|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883|1=Aidono}}. In {{ill|lt=Shōō|Q1327371|WD=Q1327371|1=Shōō (Kamakura period)}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1290|Q5552|WD=Q5552|1=1290}}), {{ill|lt=Hōjō Sadatoki|Q3136681|WD=Q3136681|1=Hōjō Sadatoki}}, regent ({{ill|lt=shikken|Q1045904|WD=Q1045904|1=Shikken}}) of the eighth shogun {{ill|lt=Prince Hisahito|Q715826|WD=Q715826|1=Prince Hisaaki}} of the {{ill|lt=Kamakura shogunate|Q736839|WD=Q736839|1=Kamakura shogunate}}, rebuilt the shrine buildings. Later, in {{ill|lt=Kan’ei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913|1=Kan'ei}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1627|Q6833|WD=Q6833|1=1627}}), the local lord {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takagi Mondo Minamoto no Masatsugu|1=UNKNOWN}} donated 900 tsubo (about 3,000 {{ill|lt=square meters|Q25343|WD=Q25343|lt=m2|1=Square metre}}) of shrine land and rebuilt the main hall. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, the shrine was also known as “Daijingū” and “Shinmeigū.” In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was ranked as a village shrine under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. In {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 12 ({{ill|lt=1923|Q2169|WD=Q2169|1=1923}}), the shrine buildings (built in {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259|1=Ansei}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1858|Q7684|WD=Q7684|1=1858}})) were completely destroyed, including the main hall, due to the {{ill|lt=Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341|WD=Q3742341|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake}}. However, they were fully restored three years later in Taishō 15 ({{ill|lt=1926|Q5259|WD=Q5259|1=1926}}) September, and remain as they are today. The temple bell at the {{ill|lt=bell tower|Q1363585|WD=Q1363585|1=Shōrō}} located just across the road west of the {{ill|lt=precincts|Q11429002|WD=Q11429002|1=keidai}} was offered in the spring of {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 20 ({{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240|1=1945}}) for material collection during the {{ill|lt=Pacific War|Q184425|WD=Q184425|1=Pacific War}}, and was not installed for some time, but was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1975|Q2479|WD=Q2479|1=1975}}. The entire shrine grounds were designated as a scenic forest, and on March 15, {{ill|lt=1974|Q2478|WD=Q2478|1=1974}}, it was designated as a {{ill|lt=Natural Environment Conservation Area|Q11612866|WD=Q11612866|1=Nature Conservation Area}}.{{cite web|publisher=Kanagawa Prefecture|url=https://www.pref.kanagawa.jp/docs/t4i/cnt/f10578/p34320.html|title=Designation status of Kanagawa Prefecture Natural Environment Conservation Areas|accessdate=2024-12-05}} === Auxiliary shrines === Within the precincts, in addition to the main shrine, there are the following {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=auxiliary shrines|1=UNKNOWN}}: * {{ill|lt=Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247|WD=Q48532247|1=Sugawara Shrine (disambiguation)}} * {{ill|lt=Inari Shrine|Q514480|WD=Q514480|1=Inari shrine}} === Festivals === * {{ill|lt=January 1|Q2150|WD=Q2150|1=January 1}} - {{ill|lt=Saitan-sai|Q11546824|WD=Q11546824|1=Saitan-sai}} * {{ill|lt=September 15|Q2846|WD=Q2846|1=September 15}} - '''{{ill|lt=Reitaisai|Q11546824|WD=Q11546824|1=Saitan-sai}}''' * The Sunday before {{ill|lt=November 15|Q2982|WD=Q2982|1=November 15}} - {{ill|lt=Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387|WD=Q1072387|1=Shichi-Go-San}} Festival * Last Sunday of November - {{ill|lt=Niiname-sai|Q11501518|WD=Q11501518|1=Niiname-no-Matsuri}} ({{ill|lt=Harvest festival|Q2304773|WD=Q2304773|1=Harvest festival}}) * {{ill|lt=December 30|Q2901|WD=Q2901|1=December 30}} - {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910|1=Ōharae-shiki}} === Reservoir === At 2009 Ōdaira, Uchimoto, there is a shrine-owned {{ill|lt=reservoir|Q112083751|WD=Q112083751|1=irrigation reservoir}} (about 150 m2). === Origin of the Place Name === One theory regarding the origin of the local place name (Uchimoto) is that it derived from “Uzumochi” (宇豆毛遅) of Utsumochi Shrine, which became corrupted into “Uchimodori.” In {{ill|lt=archaic language|Q11411592|WD=Q11411592|1=archaic language}}, both “utsu” and “mochi” are said to mean “small {{ill|lt=basin|Q813672|WD=Q813672|1=Basin (geomorphology)}},” and it is believed that the area was first called “Utsumochi” due to its topography, which later changed into “Uchimodori.” Another theory holds that when the swordsmith {{ill|lt=Masamune|Q708935|WD=Q708935|lt=Gorō Masamune|1=Masamune}} from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ebina|1=UNKNOWN}} was traveling to {{ill|lt=Kamakura|Q10939227|WD=Q10939227|1=Kamakura}}, he rested in this area and, dissatisfied with the sword he was about to present, “returned home to reforge it,” which became the origin of the place name.{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=http://www.city.fujisawa.kanagawa.jp/gosho-c/data15331.shtml |title=History of Goshomi: Origin of Place Names |accessdate=August 29, 2013 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140821214356/http://www.city.fujisawa.kanagawa.jp/gosho-c/data15331.shtml |archivedate=August 21, 2014 }} - {{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661|1=Fujisawa, Kanagawa}}[Story of Uchimoto] (Fujisawa City Machizukuri Association) === Access === * From the west exit of {{ill|lt=Shōnandai Station|Q1036357|WD=Q1036357|1=Shōnandai Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Odakyu Enoshima Line|Q1063924|WD=Q1063924|1=Odakyū Enoshima Line}}, {{ill|lt=Sotetsu Izumino Line|Q846367|WD=Q846367|1=Sōtetsu Izumino Line}}, or {{ill|lt=Yokohama Municipal Subway Blue Line|Q841360|WD=Q841360|1=Yokohama Municipal Subway Blue Line}}: ** {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Chūō Kōtsū|Q10854960|WD=Q10854960|lt=Kanachu|1=Kanagawa Chuo Kotsu}} Bus ({{ill|lt=Shō19|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892|1=Kanagawa Chuo Kotsu Ayase Branch}}): For Ayase Depot via Keio University and Miyahara; get off at “Utsumochi Shrine Entrance,” 3-minute walk. ** Kanachu Bus ({{ill|lt=Shō25|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892|1=Kanagawa Chuo Kotsu Ayase Branch}}): Twin Liner Express for Keio University; get off at “Keio Junior and Senior High School,” 10-minute walk. * From the north exit of {{ill|lt=Tsujidō Station|Q1049779|WD=Q1049779|1=Tsujidō Station}} on the {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR|1=East Japan Railway Company}} line: ** Kanachu Bus ({{ill|lt=Tsuji34|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892|1=Kanagawa Chuo Kotsu Ayase Branch}}): For Keio University; get off at “Keio University,” 15-minute walk. === Notes === There are often no staff at the {{ill|lt=shrine office|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}}, and telephone inquiries may not be possible. If you wish to receive a {{ill|lt=goshuin|Q7504772|WD=Q7504772|1=Shuin}}, it is more reliable to visit during festivals or New Year when events are being held. There are also {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=toilets|1=UNKNOWN}} installed within the shrine grounds. === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Further Reading === * Utsumochi Shrine “Shrine Bulletin Board,” Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office “Utsumochi Shrine” page, etc. === Related Articles === {{Wikicommons|Category:Utsumochi-jinja}} * {{ill|lt=List of Engishiki shrines in Sagami Province|Q11582137|WD=Q11582137|1=List of Shikinaisha in Sagami Province}} * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture|Q11588941|WD=Q11588941|1=Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office Designated Shrine}} === External Links === * https://utsumochi-jinja.com/ Utsumochi Shrine Official Website * http://www.kanagawa-jinja.or.jp/search_dtl.php4?jid=573&cd=1206054&scd=&npg=0 Utsumochi Shrine (Kanagawa Prefecture {{ill|lt=Shrine Office|Q11590703|WD=Q11590703|1=Jinjachō}}) {{shinto-stub}} {{Shinto}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Utsumochi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Utsumochi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Sagami Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:藤沢市の神社]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Sagami Province]] [[Category:神奈川県の旧郷社]] [[Category:kkkkkkkkkkkkkk]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

== Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 4021005000153 == Commons category (P373) == * Utsumochi-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '神奈川県藤沢市打戻3012', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Utsumochi-jinja}} [[JA:宇都母知神社]] {{wikidata link|Q17217329}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Utsumochi Shrine |Image = [[File:Utsumochi-jinjya 1.JPG|270px]]
Torii gate and worship hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |Location = {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661|1=Fujisawa, Kanagawa}} 3012 Uchimoto |ISO = JP-14 |Latitude degrees = 35 | Latitude minutes = 23 | Latitude seconds = 31.3 | N/S = N |Longitude degrees = 139 | Longitude minutes = 25 | Longitude seconds = 17.7 | E/W = E |Enshrined deities = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Kami|1=Wakumusubi}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} |Shrine status = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor), {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} ({{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine status|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}) |Established = Unknown |Main hall style = {{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} |Other name = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=September 15|Q2846|WD=Q2846|1=September 15}} |Map = Japan Kanagawa#Japan }} '''Utsumochi Shrine''' (うつもちじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Uchimoto, {{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661|1=Fujisawa, Kanagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}. It is regarded as one of the shrines (minor shrine) listed among the thirteen {{ill|lt=Engishiki shrine list of Sagami Province|Q11582137|WD=Q11582137|lt=Engishiki shrines|1=List of Shikinaisha in Sagami Province}} of {{ill|lt=Sagami Province|Q1053732|WD=Q1053732|1=Sagami Province}} recorded in the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'' of {{ill|lt=Engi (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Engi|1=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}).{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1273537/73?tocOpened=1 |title=''Engishiki Vol. 2 (Volumes 8, 9, 10)'', Nihon Koten Zenshū Kankōkai, Showa 4. Frame 73. ({{ill|lt=National Diet Library Digital Collections|Q117959850|WD=Q117959850|1=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}) |publisher= |accessdate=2018-09-03 }} === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Kami|1=Wakumusubi}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} === Origin and History === The exact date of the shrine’s founding is unknown, but there is a record that solemn {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=rituals|1=UNKNOWN}} were conducted in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235|WD=Q357235|1=Emperor Yūryaku}} (according to the {{ill|lt=Kōki era|Q1209728|WD=Q1209728|lt=Imperial Year|1=Japanese imperial year}} 1119Stone monument within the shrine grounds{{Refnest|group="Note"|The Utsumochi Shrine page (Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office website, viewed September 20, 2017) states {{ill|lt=495|Q30806|WD=Q30806|1=495}}.}}) (''Nihon Sōkoku {{ill|lt=Fudoki|Q1194534|WD=Q1194534|1=Fudoki}}''). Although there are no records of relocation, the same fudoki states that the shrine was located in “Utsumochi-go, Takakura District, Sagami Province.”{{Cite web|Japanese book|author= |url=http://www.kanagawa-jinja.or.jp/search_dtl.php4?jid=573&cd=1206054&scd=&npg=0 |title=Utsumochi Shrine |date=|publisher=Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office |accessdate=2017-09-20 }} In the ''Engishiki Jinmyōchō'', it is recorded as “Utsumochi Shrine, one of the six shrines in {{ill|lt=Kōza District|Q1202776|WD=Q1202776|1=Kōza District, Kanagawa}} among the thirteen shrines of Sagami Province (shikinaisha).” In {{ill|lt=Tengyō|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340|1=Tengyō}} 2 ({{ill|lt=939|Q23422|WD=Q23422|1=939}}), the spirit of Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto was transferred from {{ill|lt=Yamato Province|Q907495|WD=Q907495|1=Yamato Province}}{{ill|lt=Hatsuse|Q11396531|WD=Q11396531|lt=Hase|1=Hase}} and enshrined together in the {{ill|lt=aiden|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883|1=Aidono}}. In {{ill|lt=Shōō|Q1327371|WD=Q1327371|1=Shōō (Kamakura period)}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1290|Q5552|WD=Q5552|1=1290}}), {{ill|lt=Hōjō Sadatoki|Q3136681|WD=Q3136681|1=Hōjō Sadatoki}}, regent ({{ill|lt=shikken|Q1045904|WD=Q1045904|1=Shikken}}) of the eighth shogun {{ill|lt=Prince Hisahito|Q715826|WD=Q715826|1=Prince Hisaaki}} of the {{ill|lt=Kamakura shogunate|Q736839|WD=Q736839|1=Kamakura shogunate}}, rebuilt the shrine buildings. Later, in {{ill|lt=Kan’ei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913|1=Kan'ei}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1627|Q6833|WD=Q6833|1=1627}}), the local lord {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takagi Mondo Minamoto no Masatsugu|1=UNKNOWN}} donated 900 tsubo (about 3,000 {{ill|lt=square meters|Q25343|WD=Q25343|lt=m2|1=Square metre}}) of shrine land and rebuilt the main hall. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, the shrine was also known as “Daijingū” and “Shinmeigū.” In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was ranked as a village shrine under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. In {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 12 ({{ill|lt=1923|Q2169|WD=Q2169|1=1923}}), the shrine buildings (built in {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259|1=Ansei}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1858|Q7684|WD=Q7684|1=1858}})) were completely destroyed, including the main hall, due to the {{ill|lt=Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341|WD=Q3742341|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake}}. However, they were fully restored three years later in Taishō 15 ({{ill|lt=1926|Q5259|WD=Q5259|1=1926}}) September, and remain as they are today. The temple bell at the {{ill|lt=bell tower|Q1363585|WD=Q1363585|1=Shōrō}} located just across the road west of the {{ill|lt=precincts|Q11429002|WD=Q11429002|1=keidai}} was offered in the spring of {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 20 ({{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240|1=1945}}) for material collection during the {{ill|lt=Pacific War|Q184425|WD=Q184425|1=Pacific War}}, and was not installed for some time, but was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1975|Q2479|WD=Q2479|1=1975}}. The entire shrine grounds were designated as a scenic forest, and on March 15, {{ill|lt=1974|Q2478|WD=Q2478|1=1974}}, it was designated as a {{ill|lt=Natural Environment Conservation Area|Q11612866|WD=Q11612866|1=Nature Conservation Area}}.{{cite web|publisher=Kanagawa Prefecture|url=https://www.pref.kanagawa.jp/docs/t4i/cnt/f10578/p34320.html|title=Designation status of Kanagawa Prefecture Natural Environment Conservation Areas|accessdate=2024-12-05}} === Auxiliary shrines === Within the precincts, in addition to the main shrine, there are the following {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=auxiliary shrines|1=UNKNOWN}}: * {{ill|lt=Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247|WD=Q48532247|1=Sugawara Shrine (disambiguation)}} * {{ill|lt=Inari Shrine|Q514480|WD=Q514480|1=Inari shrine}} === Festivals === * {{ill|lt=January 1|Q2150|WD=Q2150|1=January 1}} - {{ill|lt=Saitan-sai|Q11546824|WD=Q11546824|1=Saitan-sai}} * {{ill|lt=September 15|Q2846|WD=Q2846|1=September 15}} - '''{{ill|lt=Reitaisai|Q11546824|WD=Q11546824|1=Saitan-sai}}''' * The Sunday before {{ill|lt=November 15|Q2982|WD=Q2982|1=November 15}} - {{ill|lt=Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387|WD=Q1072387|1=Shichi-Go-San}} Festival * Last Sunday of November - {{ill|lt=Niiname-sai|Q11501518|WD=Q11501518|1=Niiname-no-Matsuri}} ({{ill|lt=Harvest festival|Q2304773|WD=Q2304773|1=Harvest festival}}) * {{ill|lt=December 30|Q2901|WD=Q2901|1=December 30}} - {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910|1=Ōharae-shiki}} === Reservoir === At 2009 Ōdaira, Uchimoto, there is a shrine-owned {{ill|lt=reservoir|Q112083751|WD=Q112083751|1=irrigation reservoir}} (about 150 m2). === Origin of the Place Name === One theory regarding the origin of the local place name (Uchimoto) is that it derived from “Uzumochi” (宇豆毛遅) of Utsumochi Shrine, which became corrupted into “Uchimodori.” In {{ill|lt=archaic language|Q11411592|WD=Q11411592|1=archaic language}}, both “utsu” and “mochi” are said to mean “small {{ill|lt=basin|Q813672|WD=Q813672|1=Basin (geomorphology)}},” and it is believed that the area was first called “Utsumochi” due to its topography, which later changed into “Uchimodori.” Another theory holds that when the swordsmith {{ill|lt=Masamune|Q708935|WD=Q708935|lt=Gorō Masamune|1=Masamune}} from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ebina|1=UNKNOWN}} was traveling to {{ill|lt=Kamakura|Q10939227|WD=Q10939227|1=Kamakura}}, he rested in this area and, dissatisfied with the sword he was about to present, “returned home to reforge it,” which became the origin of the place name.{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=http://www.city.fujisawa.kanagawa.jp/gosho-c/data15331.shtml |title=History of Goshomi: Origin of Place Names |accessdate=August 29, 2013 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140821214356/http://www.city.fujisawa.kanagawa.jp/gosho-c/data15331.shtml |archivedate=August 21, 2014 }} - {{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661|1=Fujisawa, Kanagawa}}[Story of Uchimoto] (Fujisawa City Machizukuri Association) === Access === * From the west exit of {{ill|lt=Shōnandai Station|Q1036357|WD=Q1036357|1=Shōnandai Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Odakyu Enoshima Line|Q1063924|WD=Q1063924|1=Odakyū Enoshima Line}}, {{ill|lt=Sotetsu Izumino Line|Q846367|WD=Q846367|1=Sōtetsu Izumino Line}}, or {{ill|lt=Yokohama Municipal Subway Blue Line|Q841360|WD=Q841360|1=Yokohama Municipal Subway Blue Line}}: ** {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Chūō Kōtsū|Q10854960|WD=Q10854960|lt=Kanachu|1=Kanagawa Chuo Kotsu}} Bus ({{ill|lt=Shō19|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892|1=Kanagawa Chuo Kotsu Ayase Branch}}): For Ayase Depot via Keio University and Miyahara; get off at “Utsumochi Shrine Entrance,” 3-minute walk. ** Kanachu Bus ({{ill|lt=Shō25|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892|1=Kanagawa Chuo Kotsu Ayase Branch}}): Twin Liner Express for Keio University; get off at “Keio Junior and Senior High School,” 10-minute walk. * From the north exit of {{ill|lt=Tsujidō Station|Q1049779|WD=Q1049779|1=Tsujidō Station}} on the {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR|1=East Japan Railway Company}} line: ** Kanachu Bus ({{ill|lt=Tsuji34|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892|1=Kanagawa Chuo Kotsu Ayase Branch}}): For Keio University; get off at “Keio University,” 15-minute walk. === Notes === There are often no staff at the {{ill|lt=shrine office|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}}, and telephone inquiries may not be possible. If you wish to receive a {{ill|lt=goshuin|Q7504772|WD=Q7504772|1=Shuin}}, it is more reliable to visit during festivals or New Year when events are being held. There are also {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=toilets|1=UNKNOWN}} installed within the shrine grounds. === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Further Reading === * Utsumochi Shrine “Shrine Bulletin Board,” Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office “Utsumochi Shrine” page, etc. === Related Articles === {{Wikicommons|Category:Utsumochi-jinja}} * {{ill|lt=List of Engishiki shrines in Sagami Province|Q11582137|WD=Q11582137|1=List of Shikinaisha in Sagami Province}} * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture|Q11588941|WD=Q11588941|1=Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office Designated Shrine}} === External Links === * https://utsumochi-jinja.com/ Utsumochi Shrine Official Website * http://www.kanagawa-jinja.or.jp/search_dtl.php4?jid=573&cd=1206054&scd=&npg=0 Utsumochi Shrine (Kanagawa Prefecture {{ill|lt=Shrine Office|Q11590703|WD=Q11590703|1=Jinjachō}}) {{shinto-stub}} {{Shinto}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Utsumochi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Utsumochi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Sagami Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:藤沢市の神社]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Sagami Province]] [[Category:神奈川県の旧郷社]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

category|Category:Utsumochi-jinja}} [[JA:宇都母知神社]] {{wikidata link|Q17217329}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Utsumochi Shrine |Image = [[File:Utsumochi-jinjya 1.JPG|270px]]
Torii gate and worship hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |Location = {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661|1=Fujisawa, Kanagawa}} 3012 Uchimoto |ISO = JP-14 |Latitude degrees = 35 | Latitude minutes = 23 | Latitude seconds = 31.3 | N/S = N |Longitude degrees = 139 | Longitude minutes = 25 | Longitude seconds = 17.7 | E/W = E |Enshrined deities = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Kami|1=Wakumusubi}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} |Shrine status = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor), {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} ({{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine status|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}) |Established = Unknown |Main hall style = {{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} |Other name = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=September 15|Q2846|WD=Q2846|1=September 15}} |Map = Japan Kanagawa#Japan }} '''Utsumochi Shrine''' (うつもちじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Uchimoto, {{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661|1=Fujisawa, Kanagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}. It is regarded as one of the shrines (minor shrine) listed among the thirteen {{ill|lt=Engishiki shrine list of Sagami Province|Q11582137|WD=Q11582137|lt=Engishiki shrines|1=List of Shikinaisha in Sagami Province}} of {{ill|lt=Sagami Province|Q1053732|WD=Q1053732|1=Sagami Province}} recorded in the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'' of {{ill|lt=Engi (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Engi|1=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}).{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1273537/73?tocOpened=1 |title=''Engishiki Vol. 2 (Volumes 8, 9, 10)'', Nihon Koten Zenshū Kankōkai, Showa 4. Frame 73. ({{ill|lt=National Diet Library Digital Collections|Q117959850|WD=Q117959850|1=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}) |publisher= |accessdate=2018-09-03 }} === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Kami|1=Wakumusubi}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} === Origin and History === The exact date of the shrine’s founding is unknown, but there is a record that solemn {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=rituals|1=UNKNOWN}} were conducted in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235|WD=Q357235|1=Emperor Yūryaku}} (according to the {{ill|lt=Kōki era|Q1209728|WD=Q1209728|lt=Imperial Year|1=Japanese imperial year}} 1119Stone monument within the shrine grounds{{Refnest|group="Note"|The Utsumochi Shrine page (Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office website, viewed September 20, 2017) states {{ill|lt=495|Q30806|WD=Q30806|1=495}}.}}) (''Nihon Sōkoku {{ill|lt=Fudoki|Q1194534|WD=Q1194534|1=Fudoki}}''). Although there are no records of relocation, the same fudoki states that the shrine was located in “Utsumochi-go, Takakura District, Sagami Province.”{{Cite web|Japanese book|author= |url=http://www.kanagawa-jinja.or.jp/search_dtl.php4?jid=573&cd=1206054&scd=&npg=0 |title=Utsumochi Shrine |date=|publisher=Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office |accessdate=2017-09-20 }} In the ''Engishiki Jinmyōchō'', it is recorded as “Utsumochi Shrine, one of the six shrines in {{ill|lt=Kōza District|Q1202776|WD=Q1202776|1=Kōza District, Kanagawa}} among the thirteen shrines of Sagami Province (shikinaisha).” In {{ill|lt=Tengyō|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340|1=Tengyō}} 2 ({{ill|lt=939|Q23422|WD=Q23422|1=939}}), the spirit of Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto was transferred from {{ill|lt=Yamato Province|Q907495|WD=Q907495|1=Yamato Province}}{{ill|lt=Hatsuse|Q11396531|WD=Q11396531|lt=Hase|1=Hase}} and enshrined together in the {{ill|lt=aiden|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883|1=Aidono}}. In {{ill|lt=Shōō|Q1327371|WD=Q1327371|1=Shōō (Kamakura period)}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1290|Q5552|WD=Q5552|1=1290}}), {{ill|lt=Hōjō Sadatoki|Q3136681|WD=Q3136681|1=Hōjō Sadatoki}}, regent ({{ill|lt=shikken|Q1045904|WD=Q1045904|1=Shikken}}) of the eighth shogun {{ill|lt=Prince Hisahito|Q715826|WD=Q715826|1=Prince Hisaaki}} of the {{ill|lt=Kamakura shogunate|Q736839|WD=Q736839|1=Kamakura shogunate}}, rebuilt the shrine buildings. Later, in {{ill|lt=Kan’ei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913|1=Kan'ei}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1627|Q6833|WD=Q6833|1=1627}}), the local lord {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takagi Mondo Minamoto no Masatsugu|1=UNKNOWN}} donated 900 tsubo (about 3,000 {{ill|lt=square meters|Q25343|WD=Q25343|lt=m2|1=Square metre}}) of shrine land and rebuilt the main hall. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, the shrine was also known as “Daijingū” and “Shinmeigū.” In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was ranked as a village shrine under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. In {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 12 ({{ill|lt=1923|Q2169|WD=Q2169|1=1923}}), the shrine buildings (built in {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259|1=Ansei}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1858|Q7684|WD=Q7684|1=1858}})) were completely destroyed, including the main hall, due to the {{ill|lt=Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341|WD=Q3742341|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake}}. However, they were fully restored three years later in Taishō 15 ({{ill|lt=1926|Q5259|WD=Q5259|1=1926}}) September, and remain as they are today. The temple bell at the {{ill|lt=bell tower|Q1363585|WD=Q1363585|1=Shōrō}} located just across the road west of the {{ill|lt=precincts|Q11429002|WD=Q11429002|1=keidai}} was offered in the spring of {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 20 ({{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240|1=1945}}) for material collection during the {{ill|lt=Pacific War|Q184425|WD=Q184425|1=Pacific War}}, and was not installed for some time, but was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1975|Q2479|WD=Q2479|1=1975}}. The entire shrine grounds were designated as a scenic forest, and on March 15, {{ill|lt=1974|Q2478|WD=Q2478|1=1974}}, it was designated as a {{ill|lt=Natural Environment Conservation Area|Q11612866|WD=Q11612866|1=Nature Conservation Area}}.{{cite web|publisher=Kanagawa Prefecture|url=https://www.pref.kanagawa.jp/docs/t4i/cnt/f10578/p34320.html|title=Designation status of Kanagawa Prefecture Natural Environment Conservation Areas|accessdate=2024-12-05}} === Auxiliary shrines === Within the precincts, in addition to the main shrine, there are the following {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=auxiliary shrines|1=UNKNOWN}}: * {{ill|lt=Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247|WD=Q48532247|1=Sugawara Shrine (disambiguation)}} * {{ill|lt=Inari Shrine|Q514480|WD=Q514480|1=Inari shrine}} === Festivals === * {{ill|lt=January 1|Q2150|WD=Q2150|1=January 1}} - {{ill|lt=Saitan-sai|Q11546824|WD=Q11546824|1=Saitan-sai}} * {{ill|lt=September 15|Q2846|WD=Q2846|1=September 15}} - '''{{ill|lt=Reitaisai|Q11546824|WD=Q11546824|1=Saitan-sai}}''' * The Sunday before {{ill|lt=November 15|Q2982|WD=Q2982|1=November 15}} - {{ill|lt=Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387|WD=Q1072387|1=Shichi-Go-San}} Festival * Last Sunday of November - {{ill|lt=Niiname-sai|Q11501518|WD=Q11501518|1=Niiname-no-Matsuri}} ({{ill|lt=Harvest festival|Q2304773|WD=Q2304773|1=Harvest festival}}) * {{ill|lt=December 30|Q2901|WD=Q2901|1=December 30}} - {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910|1=Ōharae-shiki}} === Reservoir === At 2009 Ōdaira, Uchimoto, there is a shrine-owned {{ill|lt=reservoir|Q112083751|WD=Q112083751|1=irrigation reservoir}} (about 150 m2). === Origin of the Place Name === One theory regarding the origin of the local place name (Uchimoto) is that it derived from “Uzumochi” (宇豆毛遅) of Utsumochi Shrine, which became corrupted into “Uchimodori.” In {{ill|lt=archaic language|Q11411592|WD=Q11411592|1=archaic language}}, both “utsu” and “mochi” are said to mean “small {{ill|lt=basin|Q813672|WD=Q813672|1=Basin (geomorphology)}},” and it is believed that the area was first called “Utsumochi” due to its topography, which later changed into “Uchimodori.” Another theory holds that when the swordsmith {{ill|lt=Masamune|Q708935|WD=Q708935|lt=Gorō Masamune|1=Masamune}} from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ebina|1=UNKNOWN}} was traveling to {{ill|lt=Kamakura|Q10939227|WD=Q10939227|1=Kamakura}}, he rested in this area and, dissatisfied with the sword he was about to present, “returned home to reforge it,” which became the origin of the place name.{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=http://www.city.fujisawa.kanagawa.jp/gosho-c/data15331.shtml |title=History of Goshomi: Origin of Place Names |accessdate=August 29, 2013 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140821214356/http://www.city.fujisawa.kanagawa.jp/gosho-c/data15331.shtml |archivedate=August 21, 2014 }} - {{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661|1=Fujisawa, Kanagawa}}[Story of Uchimoto] (Fujisawa City Machizukuri Association) === Access === * From the west exit of {{ill|lt=Shōnandai Station|Q1036357|WD=Q1036357|1=Shōnandai Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Odakyu Enoshima Line|Q1063924|WD=Q1063924|1=Odakyū Enoshima Line}}, {{ill|lt=Sotetsu Izumino Line|Q846367|WD=Q846367|1=Sōtetsu Izumino Line}}, or {{ill|lt=Yokohama Municipal Subway Blue Line|Q841360|WD=Q841360|1=Yokohama Municipal Subway Blue Line}}: ** {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Chūō Kōtsū|Q10854960|WD=Q10854960|lt=Kanachu|1=Kanagawa Chuo Kotsu}} Bus ({{ill|lt=Shō19|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892|1=Kanagawa Chuo Kotsu Ayase Branch}}): For Ayase Depot via Keio University and Miyahara; get off at “Utsumochi Shrine Entrance,” 3-minute walk. ** Kanachu Bus ({{ill|lt=Shō25|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892|1=Kanagawa Chuo Kotsu Ayase Branch}}): Twin Liner Express for Keio University; get off at “Keio Junior and Senior High School,” 10-minute walk. * From the north exit of {{ill|lt=Tsujidō Station|Q1049779|WD=Q1049779|1=Tsujidō Station}} on the {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR|1=East Japan Railway Company}} line: ** Kanachu Bus ({{ill|lt=Tsuji34|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892|1=Kanagawa Chuo Kotsu Ayase Branch}}): For Keio University; get off at “Keio University,” 15-minute walk. === Notes === There are often no staff at the {{ill|lt=shrine office|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}}, and telephone inquiries may not be possible. If you wish to receive a {{ill|lt=goshuin|Q7504772|WD=Q7504772|1=Shuin}}, it is more reliable to visit during festivals or New Year when events are being held. There are also {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=toilets|1=UNKNOWN}} installed within the shrine grounds. === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Further Reading === * Utsumochi Shrine “Shrine Bulletin Board,” Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office “Utsumochi Shrine” page, etc. === Related Articles === {{Wikicommons|Category:Utsumochi-jinja}} * {{ill|lt=List of Engishiki shrines in Sagami Province|Q11582137|WD=Q11582137|1=List of Shikinaisha in Sagami Province}} * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture|Q11588941|WD=Q11588941|1=Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office Designated Shrine}} === External Links === * https://utsumochi-jinja.com/ Utsumochi Shrine Official Website * http://www.kanagawa-jinja.or.jp/search_dtl.php4?jid=573&cd=1206054&scd=&npg=0 Utsumochi Shrine (Kanagawa Prefecture {{ill|lt=Shrine Office|Q11590703|WD=Q11590703|1=Jinjachō}}) {{shinto-stub}} {{Shinto}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Utsumochi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Utsumochi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Sagami Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:藤沢市の神社]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Sagami Province]] [[Category:神奈川県の旧郷社]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

== Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 4021005000153 == Commons category (P373) == * Utsumochi-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '神奈川県藤沢市打戻3012', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Utsumochi-jinja}} [[JA:宇都母知神社]] {{wikidata link|Q17217329}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Utsumochi Shrine |Image = [[File:Utsumochi-jinjya 1.JPG|270px]]
Torii gate and worship hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |Location = {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661|1=Fujisawa, Kanagawa}} 3012 Uchimoto |ISO = JP-14 |Latitude degrees = 35 | Latitude minutes = 23 | Latitude seconds = 31.3 | N/S = N |Longitude degrees = 139 | Longitude minutes = 25 | Longitude seconds = 17.7 | E/W = E |Enshrined deities = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Kami|1=Wakumusubi}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} |Shrine status = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor), {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} ({{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine status|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}) |Established = Unknown |Main hall style = {{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} |Other name = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=September 15|Q2846|WD=Q2846|1=September 15}} |Map = Japan Kanagawa#Japan }} '''Utsumochi Shrine''' (うつもちじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Uchimoto, {{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661|1=Fujisawa, Kanagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}. It is regarded as one of the shrines (minor shrine) listed among the thirteen {{ill|lt=Engishiki shrine list of Sagami Province|Q11582137|WD=Q11582137|lt=Engishiki shrines|1=List of Shikinaisha in Sagami Province}} of {{ill|lt=Sagami Province|Q1053732|WD=Q1053732|1=Sagami Province}} recorded in the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'' of {{ill|lt=Engi (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Engi|1=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}).{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1273537/73?tocOpened=1 |title=''Engishiki Vol. 2 (Volumes 8, 9, 10)'', Nihon Koten Zenshū Kankōkai, Showa 4. Frame 73. ({{ill|lt=National Diet Library Digital Collections|Q117959850|WD=Q117959850|1=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}) |publisher= |accessdate=2018-09-03 }} === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Kami|1=Wakumusubi}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} === Origin and History === The exact date of the shrine’s founding is unknown, but there is a record that solemn {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=rituals|1=UNKNOWN}} were conducted in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235|WD=Q357235|1=Emperor Yūryaku}} (according to the {{ill|lt=Kōki era|Q1209728|WD=Q1209728|lt=Imperial Year|1=Japanese imperial year}} 1119Stone monument within the shrine grounds{{Refnest|group="Note"|The Utsumochi Shrine page (Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office website, viewed September 20, 2017) states {{ill|lt=495|Q30806|WD=Q30806|1=495}}.}}) (''Nihon Sōkoku {{ill|lt=Fudoki|Q1194534|WD=Q1194534|1=Fudoki}}''). Although there are no records of relocation, the same fudoki states that the shrine was located in “Utsumochi-go, Takakura District, Sagami Province.”{{Cite web|Japanese book|author= |url=http://www.kanagawa-jinja.or.jp/search_dtl.php4?jid=573&cd=1206054&scd=&npg=0 |title=Utsumochi Shrine |date=|publisher=Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office |accessdate=2017-09-20 }} In the ''Engishiki Jinmyōchō'', it is recorded as “Utsumochi Shrine, one of the six shrines in {{ill|lt=Kōza District|Q1202776|WD=Q1202776|1=Kōza District, Kanagawa}} among the thirteen shrines of Sagami Province (shikinaisha).” In {{ill|lt=Tengyō|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340|1=Tengyō}} 2 ({{ill|lt=939|Q23422|WD=Q23422|1=939}}), the spirit of Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto was transferred from {{ill|lt=Yamato Province|Q907495|WD=Q907495|1=Yamato Province}}{{ill|lt=Hatsuse|Q11396531|WD=Q11396531|lt=Hase|1=Hase}} and enshrined together in the {{ill|lt=aiden|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883|1=Aidono}}. In {{ill|lt=Shōō|Q1327371|WD=Q1327371|1=Shōō (Kamakura period)}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1290|Q5552|WD=Q5552|1=1290}}), {{ill|lt=Hōjō Sadatoki|Q3136681|WD=Q3136681|1=Hōjō Sadatoki}}, regent ({{ill|lt=shikken|Q1045904|WD=Q1045904|1=Shikken}}) of the eighth shogun {{ill|lt=Prince Hisahito|Q715826|WD=Q715826|1=Prince Hisaaki}} of the {{ill|lt=Kamakura shogunate|Q736839|WD=Q736839|1=Kamakura shogunate}}, rebuilt the shrine buildings. Later, in {{ill|lt=Kan’ei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913|1=Kan'ei}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1627|Q6833|WD=Q6833|1=1627}}), the local lord {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takagi Mondo Minamoto no Masatsugu|1=UNKNOWN}} donated 900 tsubo (about 3,000 {{ill|lt=square meters|Q25343|WD=Q25343|lt=m2|1=Square metre}}) of shrine land and rebuilt the main hall. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, the shrine was also known as “Daijingū” and “Shinmeigū.” In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was ranked as a village shrine under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. In {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 12 ({{ill|lt=1923|Q2169|WD=Q2169|1=1923}}), the shrine buildings (built in {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259|1=Ansei}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1858|Q7684|WD=Q7684|1=1858}})) were completely destroyed, including the main hall, due to the {{ill|lt=Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341|WD=Q3742341|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake}}. However, they were fully restored three years later in Taishō 15 ({{ill|lt=1926|Q5259|WD=Q5259|1=1926}}) September, and remain as they are today. The temple bell at the {{ill|lt=bell tower|Q1363585|WD=Q1363585|1=Shōrō}} located just across the road west of the {{ill|lt=precincts|Q11429002|WD=Q11429002|1=keidai}} was offered in the spring of {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 20 ({{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240|1=1945}}) for material collection during the {{ill|lt=Pacific War|Q184425|WD=Q184425|1=Pacific War}}, and was not installed for some time, but was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1975|Q2479|WD=Q2479|1=1975}}. The entire shrine grounds were designated as a scenic forest, and on March 15, {{ill|lt=1974|Q2478|WD=Q2478|1=1974}}, it was designated as a {{ill|lt=Natural Environment Conservation Area|Q11612866|WD=Q11612866|1=Nature Conservation Area}}.{{cite web|publisher=Kanagawa Prefecture|url=https://www.pref.kanagawa.jp/docs/t4i/cnt/f10578/p34320.html|title=Designation status of Kanagawa Prefecture Natural Environment Conservation Areas|accessdate=2024-12-05}} === Auxiliary shrines === Within the precincts, in addition to the main shrine, there are the following {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=auxiliary shrines|1=UNKNOWN}}: * {{ill|lt=Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247|WD=Q48532247|1=Sugawara Shrine (disambiguation)}} * {{ill|lt=Inari Shrine|Q514480|WD=Q514480|1=Inari shrine}} === Festivals === * {{ill|lt=January 1|Q2150|WD=Q2150|1=January 1}} - {{ill|lt=Saitan-sai|Q11546824|WD=Q11546824|1=Saitan-sai}} * {{ill|lt=September 15|Q2846|WD=Q2846|1=September 15}} - '''{{ill|lt=Reitaisai|Q11546824|WD=Q11546824|1=Saitan-sai}}''' * The Sunday before {{ill|lt=November 15|Q2982|WD=Q2982|1=November 15}} - {{ill|lt=Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387|WD=Q1072387|1=Shichi-Go-San}} Festival * Last Sunday of November - {{ill|lt=Niiname-sai|Q11501518|WD=Q11501518|1=Niiname-no-Matsuri}} ({{ill|lt=Harvest festival|Q2304773|WD=Q2304773|1=Harvest festival}}) * {{ill|lt=December 30|Q2901|WD=Q2901|1=December 30}} - {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910|1=Ōharae-shiki}} === Reservoir === At 2009 Ōdaira, Uchimoto, there is a shrine-owned {{ill|lt=reservoir|Q112083751|WD=Q112083751|1=irrigation reservoir}} (about 150 m2). === Origin of the Place Name === One theory regarding the origin of the local place name (Uchimoto) is that it derived from “Uzumochi” (宇豆毛遅) of Utsumochi Shrine, which became corrupted into “Uchimodori.” In {{ill|lt=archaic language|Q11411592|WD=Q11411592|1=archaic language}}, both “utsu” and “mochi” are said to mean “small {{ill|lt=basin|Q813672|WD=Q813672|1=Basin (geomorphology)}},” and it is believed that the area was first called “Utsumochi” due to its topography, which later changed into “Uchimodori.” Another theory holds that when the swordsmith {{ill|lt=Masamune|Q708935|WD=Q708935|lt=Gorō Masamune|1=Masamune}} from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ebina|1=UNKNOWN}} was traveling to {{ill|lt=Kamakura|Q10939227|WD=Q10939227|1=Kamakura}}, he rested in this area and, dissatisfied with the sword he was about to present, “returned home to reforge it,” which became the origin of the place name.{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=http://www.city.fujisawa.kanagawa.jp/gosho-c/data15331.shtml |title=History of Goshomi: Origin of Place Names |accessdate=August 29, 2013 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140821214356/http://www.city.fujisawa.kanagawa.jp/gosho-c/data15331.shtml |archivedate=August 21, 2014 }} - {{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661|1=Fujisawa, Kanagawa}}[Story of Uchimoto] (Fujisawa City Machizukuri Association) === Access === * From the west exit of {{ill|lt=Shōnandai Station|Q1036357|WD=Q1036357|1=Shōnandai Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Odakyu Enoshima Line|Q1063924|WD=Q1063924|1=Odakyū Enoshima Line}}, {{ill|lt=Sotetsu Izumino Line|Q846367|WD=Q846367|1=Sōtetsu Izumino Line}}, or {{ill|lt=Yokohama Municipal Subway Blue Line|Q841360|WD=Q841360|1=Yokohama Municipal Subway Blue Line}}: ** {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Chūō Kōtsū|Q10854960|WD=Q10854960|lt=Kanachu|1=Kanagawa Chuo Kotsu}} Bus ({{ill|lt=Shō19|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892|1=Kanagawa Chuo Kotsu Ayase Branch}}): For Ayase Depot via Keio University and Miyahara; get off at “Utsumochi Shrine Entrance,” 3-minute walk. ** Kanachu Bus ({{ill|lt=Shō25|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892|1=Kanagawa Chuo Kotsu Ayase Branch}}): Twin Liner Express for Keio University; get off at “Keio Junior and Senior High School,” 10-minute walk. * From the north exit of {{ill|lt=Tsujidō Station|Q1049779|WD=Q1049779|1=Tsujidō Station}} on the {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR|1=East Japan Railway Company}} line: ** Kanachu Bus ({{ill|lt=Tsuji34|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892|1=Kanagawa Chuo Kotsu Ayase Branch}}): For Keio University; get off at “Keio University,” 15-minute walk. === Notes === There are often no staff at the {{ill|lt=shrine office|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}}, and telephone inquiries may not be possible. If you wish to receive a {{ill|lt=goshuin|Q7504772|WD=Q7504772|1=Shuin}}, it is more reliable to visit during festivals or New Year when events are being held. There are also {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=toilets|1=UNKNOWN}} installed within the shrine grounds. === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Further Reading === * Utsumochi Shrine “Shrine Bulletin Board,” Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office “Utsumochi Shrine” page, etc. === Related Articles === {{Wikicommons|Category:Utsumochi-jinja}} * {{ill|lt=List of Engishiki shrines in Sagami Province|Q11582137|WD=Q11582137|1=List of Shikinaisha in Sagami Province}} * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture|Q11588941|WD=Q11588941|1=Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office Designated Shrine}} === External Links === * https://utsumochi-jinja.com/ Utsumochi Shrine Official Website * http://www.kanagawa-jinja.or.jp/search_dtl.php4?jid=573&cd=1206054&scd=&npg=0 Utsumochi Shrine (Kanagawa Prefecture {{ill|lt=Shrine Office|Q11590703|WD=Q11590703|1=Jinjachō}}) {{shinto-stub}} {{Shinto}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Utsumochi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Utsumochi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Sagami Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:藤沢市の神社]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Sagami Province]] [[Category:神奈川県の旧郷社]]

category|Category:Utsumochi-jinja}} [[JA:宇都母知神社]] {{wikidata link|Q17217329}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Utsumochi Shrine |Image = [[File:Utsumochi-jinjya 1.JPG|270px]]
Torii gate and worship hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |Location = {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661|1=Fujisawa, Kanagawa}} 3012 Uchimoto |ISO = JP-14 |Latitude degrees = 35 | Latitude minutes = 23 | Latitude seconds = 31.3 | N/S = N |Longitude degrees = 139 | Longitude minutes = 25 | Longitude seconds = 17.7 | E/W = E |Enshrined deities = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Kami|1=Wakumusubi}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} |Shrine status = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor), {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} ({{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine status|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}) |Established = Unknown |Main hall style = {{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} |Other name = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=September 15|Q2846|WD=Q2846|1=September 15}} |Map = Japan Kanagawa#Japan }} '''Utsumochi Shrine''' (うつもちじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Uchimoto, {{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661|1=Fujisawa, Kanagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}. It is regarded as one of the shrines (minor shrine) listed among the thirteen {{ill|lt=Engishiki shrine list of Sagami Province|Q11582137|WD=Q11582137|lt=Engishiki shrines|1=List of Shikinaisha in Sagami Province}} of {{ill|lt=Sagami Province|Q1053732|WD=Q1053732|1=Sagami Province}} recorded in the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'' of {{ill|lt=Engi (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Engi|1=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}).{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1273537/73?tocOpened=1 |title=''Engishiki Vol. 2 (Volumes 8, 9, 10)'', Nihon Koten Zenshū Kankōkai, Showa 4. Frame 73. ({{ill|lt=National Diet Library Digital Collections|Q117959850|WD=Q117959850|1=National Diet Library Digital Collections}}) |publisher= |accessdate=2018-09-03 }} === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Kami|1=Wakumusubi}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} === Origin and History === The exact date of the shrine’s founding is unknown, but there is a record that solemn {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=rituals|1=UNKNOWN}} were conducted in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235|WD=Q357235|1=Emperor Yūryaku}} (according to the {{ill|lt=Kōki era|Q1209728|WD=Q1209728|lt=Imperial Year|1=Japanese imperial year}} 1119Stone monument within the shrine grounds{{Refnest|group="Note"|The Utsumochi Shrine page (Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office website, viewed September 20, 2017) states {{ill|lt=495|Q30806|WD=Q30806|1=495}}.}}) (''Nihon Sōkoku {{ill|lt=Fudoki|Q1194534|WD=Q1194534|1=Fudoki}}''). Although there are no records of relocation, the same fudoki states that the shrine was located in “Utsumochi-go, Takakura District, Sagami Province.”{{Cite web|Japanese book|author= |url=http://www.kanagawa-jinja.or.jp/search_dtl.php4?jid=573&cd=1206054&scd=&npg=0 |title=Utsumochi Shrine |date=|publisher=Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office |accessdate=2017-09-20 }} In the ''Engishiki Jinmyōchō'', it is recorded as “Utsumochi Shrine, one of the six shrines in {{ill|lt=Kōza District|Q1202776|WD=Q1202776|1=Kōza District, Kanagawa}} among the thirteen shrines of Sagami Province (shikinaisha).” In {{ill|lt=Tengyō|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340|1=Tengyō}} 2 ({{ill|lt=939|Q23422|WD=Q23422|1=939}}), the spirit of Wakahinakabe-no-Mikoto was transferred from {{ill|lt=Yamato Province|Q907495|WD=Q907495|1=Yamato Province}}{{ill|lt=Hatsuse|Q11396531|WD=Q11396531|lt=Hase|1=Hase}} and enshrined together in the {{ill|lt=aiden|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883|1=Aidono}}. In {{ill|lt=Shōō|Q1327371|WD=Q1327371|1=Shōō (Kamakura period)}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1290|Q5552|WD=Q5552|1=1290}}), {{ill|lt=Hōjō Sadatoki|Q3136681|WD=Q3136681|1=Hōjō Sadatoki}}, regent ({{ill|lt=shikken|Q1045904|WD=Q1045904|1=Shikken}}) of the eighth shogun {{ill|lt=Prince Hisahito|Q715826|WD=Q715826|1=Prince Hisaaki}} of the {{ill|lt=Kamakura shogunate|Q736839|WD=Q736839|1=Kamakura shogunate}}, rebuilt the shrine buildings. Later, in {{ill|lt=Kan’ei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913|1=Kan'ei}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1627|Q6833|WD=Q6833|1=1627}}), the local lord {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takagi Mondo Minamoto no Masatsugu|1=UNKNOWN}} donated 900 tsubo (about 3,000 {{ill|lt=square meters|Q25343|WD=Q25343|lt=m2|1=Square metre}}) of shrine land and rebuilt the main hall. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, the shrine was also known as “Daijingū” and “Shinmeigū.” In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was ranked as a village shrine under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. In {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 12 ({{ill|lt=1923|Q2169|WD=Q2169|1=1923}}), the shrine buildings (built in {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259|1=Ansei}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1858|Q7684|WD=Q7684|1=1858}})) were completely destroyed, including the main hall, due to the {{ill|lt=Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341|WD=Q3742341|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake}}. However, they were fully restored three years later in Taishō 15 ({{ill|lt=1926|Q5259|WD=Q5259|1=1926}}) September, and remain as they are today. The temple bell at the {{ill|lt=bell tower|Q1363585|WD=Q1363585|1=Shōrō}} located just across the road west of the {{ill|lt=precincts|Q11429002|WD=Q11429002|1=keidai}} was offered in the spring of {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 20 ({{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240|1=1945}}) for material collection during the {{ill|lt=Pacific War|Q184425|WD=Q184425|1=Pacific War}}, and was not installed for some time, but was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1975|Q2479|WD=Q2479|1=1975}}. The entire shrine grounds were designated as a scenic forest, and on March 15, {{ill|lt=1974|Q2478|WD=Q2478|1=1974}}, it was designated as a {{ill|lt=Natural Environment Conservation Area|Q11612866|WD=Q11612866|1=Nature Conservation Area}}.{{cite web|publisher=Kanagawa Prefecture|url=https://www.pref.kanagawa.jp/docs/t4i/cnt/f10578/p34320.html|title=Designation status of Kanagawa Prefecture Natural Environment Conservation Areas|accessdate=2024-12-05}} === Auxiliary shrines === Within the precincts, in addition to the main shrine, there are the following {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=auxiliary shrines|1=UNKNOWN}}: * {{ill|lt=Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247|WD=Q48532247|1=Sugawara Shrine (disambiguation)}} * {{ill|lt=Inari Shrine|Q514480|WD=Q514480|1=Inari shrine}} === Festivals === * {{ill|lt=January 1|Q2150|WD=Q2150|1=January 1}} - {{ill|lt=Saitan-sai|Q11546824|WD=Q11546824|1=Saitan-sai}} * {{ill|lt=September 15|Q2846|WD=Q2846|1=September 15}} - '''{{ill|lt=Reitaisai|Q11546824|WD=Q11546824|1=Saitan-sai}}''' * The Sunday before {{ill|lt=November 15|Q2982|WD=Q2982|1=November 15}} - {{ill|lt=Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387|WD=Q1072387|1=Shichi-Go-San}} Festival * Last Sunday of November - {{ill|lt=Niiname-sai|Q11501518|WD=Q11501518|1=Niiname-no-Matsuri}} ({{ill|lt=Harvest festival|Q2304773|WD=Q2304773|1=Harvest festival}}) * {{ill|lt=December 30|Q2901|WD=Q2901|1=December 30}} - {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910|1=Ōharae-shiki}} === Reservoir === At 2009 Ōdaira, Uchimoto, there is a shrine-owned {{ill|lt=reservoir|Q112083751|WD=Q112083751|1=irrigation reservoir}} (about 150 m2). === Origin of the Place Name === One theory regarding the origin of the local place name (Uchimoto) is that it derived from “Uzumochi” (宇豆毛遅) of Utsumochi Shrine, which became corrupted into “Uchimodori.” In {{ill|lt=archaic language|Q11411592|WD=Q11411592|1=archaic language}}, both “utsu” and “mochi” are said to mean “small {{ill|lt=basin|Q813672|WD=Q813672|1=Basin (geomorphology)}},” and it is believed that the area was first called “Utsumochi” due to its topography, which later changed into “Uchimodori.” Another theory holds that when the swordsmith {{ill|lt=Masamune|Q708935|WD=Q708935|lt=Gorō Masamune|1=Masamune}} from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ebina|1=UNKNOWN}} was traveling to {{ill|lt=Kamakura|Q10939227|WD=Q10939227|1=Kamakura}}, he rested in this area and, dissatisfied with the sword he was about to present, “returned home to reforge it,” which became the origin of the place name.{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=http://www.city.fujisawa.kanagawa.jp/gosho-c/data15331.shtml |title=History of Goshomi: Origin of Place Names |accessdate=August 29, 2013 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140821214356/http://www.city.fujisawa.kanagawa.jp/gosho-c/data15331.shtml |archivedate=August 21, 2014 }} - {{ill|lt=Fujisawa City|Q386661|WD=Q386661|1=Fujisawa, Kanagawa}}[Story of Uchimoto] (Fujisawa City Machizukuri Association) === Access === * From the west exit of {{ill|lt=Shōnandai Station|Q1036357|WD=Q1036357|1=Shōnandai Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Odakyu Enoshima Line|Q1063924|WD=Q1063924|1=Odakyū Enoshima Line}}, {{ill|lt=Sotetsu Izumino Line|Q846367|WD=Q846367|1=Sōtetsu Izumino Line}}, or {{ill|lt=Yokohama Municipal Subway Blue Line|Q841360|WD=Q841360|1=Yokohama Municipal Subway Blue Line}}: ** {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Chūō Kōtsū|Q10854960|WD=Q10854960|lt=Kanachu|1=Kanagawa Chuo Kotsu}} Bus ({{ill|lt=Shō19|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892|1=Kanagawa Chuo Kotsu Ayase Branch}}): For Ayase Depot via Keio University and Miyahara; get off at “Utsumochi Shrine Entrance,” 3-minute walk. ** Kanachu Bus ({{ill|lt=Shō25|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892|1=Kanagawa Chuo Kotsu Ayase Branch}}): Twin Liner Express for Keio University; get off at “Keio Junior and Senior High School,” 10-minute walk. * From the north exit of {{ill|lt=Tsujidō Station|Q1049779|WD=Q1049779|1=Tsujidō Station}} on the {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR|1=East Japan Railway Company}} line: ** Kanachu Bus ({{ill|lt=Tsuji34|Q11588892|WD=Q11588892|1=Kanagawa Chuo Kotsu Ayase Branch}}): For Keio University; get off at “Keio University,” 15-minute walk. === Notes === There are often no staff at the {{ill|lt=shrine office|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}}, and telephone inquiries may not be possible. If you wish to receive a {{ill|lt=goshuin|Q7504772|WD=Q7504772|1=Shuin}}, it is more reliable to visit during festivals or New Year when events are being held. There are also {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=toilets|1=UNKNOWN}} installed within the shrine grounds. === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Further Reading === * Utsumochi Shrine “Shrine Bulletin Board,” Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office “Utsumochi Shrine” page, etc. === Related Articles === {{Wikicommons|Category:Utsumochi-jinja}} * {{ill|lt=List of Engishiki shrines in Sagami Province|Q11582137|WD=Q11582137|1=List of Shikinaisha in Sagami Province}} * {{ill|lt=List of shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture|Q11588941|WD=Q11588941|1=Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office Designated Shrine}} === External Links === * https://utsumochi-jinja.com/ Utsumochi Shrine Official Website * http://www.kanagawa-jinja.or.jp/search_dtl.php4?jid=573&cd=1206054&scd=&npg=0 Utsumochi Shrine (Kanagawa Prefecture {{ill|lt=Shrine Office|Q11590703|WD=Q11590703|1=Jinjachō}}) {{shinto-stub}} {{Shinto}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Utsumochi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Utsumochi Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Sagami Province]] [[Category:藤沢市の神社]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Sagami Province]] [[Category:神奈川県の旧郷社]]
